result.question: How does age affect the ability to comprehend accelerated/time-compressed/fast speech ("speed listening") across the full human lifespan? Cover: (1) the developmental maturation curve in childhood/adolescence and how children compare to young adults; (2) the aging decline in older adults (50s-80s), its steepness and onset age; (3) mechanisms disentangling peripheral hearing loss (presbycusis) from central auditory temporal processing, cognitive/perceptual processing speed, and working-memory/phonological-loop capacity, including whether the age penalty persists after controlling for audibility; (4) the peak age and plateau width for accelerated-speech comprehension; and (5) trainability/plasticity across age, including whether older adults can improve and how age of onset of blindness (early- vs late-blind) modulates the ultra-fast-speech advantage and visual-cortex plasticity. Provide quantitative thresholds (syllables/sec, words/min, % compression) and effect sizes by age band, prefer peer-reviewed sources, and flag where peripheral hearing loss is/isn't controlled.

angles.label: Aging decline + audibility-controlled mechanisms (primary/academic)
angles.query: time-compressed speech comprehension older adults age decline central auditory temporal processing controlling for hearing loss audibility presbycusis
angles.rationale: Targets the core aging question (angle 2) and the mechanism dissociation (angle 3) simultaneously — peer-reviewed studies that statistically control for pure-tone audibility are the highest-signal sources for separating peripheral from central/cognitive effects and quantifying onset/steepness of decline.

angles.label: Developmental maturation curve in children/adolescents
angles.query: developmental maturation time-compressed accelerated speech perception comprehension children adolescents versus young adults rate of speech processing age
angles.rationale: Covers angle 1 — surfaces child/adolescent psycholinguistic and audiology studies establishing at what age rapid-speech processing matures and how children's compression thresholds compare to young adults.

angles.label: Quantitative thresholds: syllables/sec, WPM, % compression by age band
angles.query: maximum intelligibility time-compressed speech rate syllables per second words per minute compression ratio threshold by age group quantitative
angles.rationale: Directly serves the quantitative-figures requirement and angles 2/4 — finds papers reporting numeric intelligibility limits and peak/plateau rates that can be benchmarked against the 13 syllables/second context figure.

angles.label: Cognitive processing speed & working-memory/phonological-loop mediators
angles.query: cognitive processing speed working memory phonological loop capacity mediates age differences rapid speech understanding effect size
angles.rationale: Isolates the cognitive arm of angle 3 — which mechanism dominates the age effect — by targeting individual-differences and mediation/regression studies reporting effect sizes for processing speed vs working memory.

angles.label: Training/plasticity in older adults & blindness onset (early- vs late-blind ultrafast speech)
angles.query: training plasticity older adults improve time-compressed speech comprehension early-blind late-blind ultra-fast speech visual cortex onset of blindness
angles.rationale: Covers angle 5 — combines auditory training/plasticity-in-aging literature with the blind ultra-fast-speech neuroimaging work (V1 recruitment, early- vs late-blind differences) that the question specifically calls out as a plasticity/age-of-onset modulator.

result.summary: Five non-overlapping angles map onto the question's five sub-parts: an audibility-controlled aging/mechanism query (the analytical crux), a developmental-curve query, a quantitative-thresholds query for syllables/sec and WPM benchmarking, a cognitive-mediators query for effect sizes, and a training-plus-blindness-plasticity query. Together they span childhood through old age, separate peripheral from central/cognitive causes, and capture both the peak/plateau and trainability dimensions while prioritizing peer-reviewed quantitative evidence.

results.title: Time-Compressed Speech Identification Is Predicted by Auditory Neural Processing, Perceptuomotor Speed, and Executive Functioning in Younger and Older Listeners (Smith et al.)
results.snippet: Most quantitatively complete and mechanism-defining source. Reports intelligibility by age band at fixed compression (original ~200 wpm): 40% -> younger 95.5% vs older 92.7% (n.s.); 50% -> 86.8% vs 79.5%; 60% -> 74.0% vs 53.7% (a 20.3-point age gap). Crucially controls audibility (all <=25 dB HL, presented at 90 dB SPL) and finds adding audiometric thresholds did NOT improve model fit -- presbycusis does not mediate the age effect. Instead perceptuomotor (processing) speed MEDIATES the age difference (age becomes non-significant once it is entered), with auditory neural processing (P1 latency) and executive function as independent predictors. Directly answers angle 3 (central/cognitive dominates over peripheral) and supplies the % compression x age numbers.

results.title: The recognition of time-compressed speech as a function of age in listeners with cochlear implants or normal hearing (Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience, 2022)
results.snippet: Best source for syllables/second benchmarks against the 13 syl/s context figure. States the upper limit for young normal-hearing listeners to perceive 50% of time-compressed speech is ~11-16 syllables/sec; NH compression thresholds ~16.7 syl/s vs cochlear-implant listeners rarely above 10 syl/s. Maps compression ratio to rate (0%≈3.7, 20%≈4.6, 40%≈6.4, 60%≈10 syl/s for that material) and shows continuous, not threshold-based, age decline with larger decrements for middle-aged/older listeners emerging before age 65. Serves the quantitative-thresholds angle and angles 2/4 directly.

results.title: Comprehension of time-compressed speech: effects of age and speech complexity (Wingfield/Gordon-Salant lineage; PubMed)
results.snippet: Classic reference establishing the % compression x age interaction: comprehension is largely preserved across age until compression becomes severe (decline emerging around 60% compression), where the task shifts from perceptual to cognitive (top-down inference) and older adults show disproportionate decrements. Anchors the peak/plateau characterization (angle 4) and the speech-complexity interaction. Foundational, frequently cited; complements the newer quantitative papers.

results.title: Ongoing maturation in the time-compressed speech test (PMC)
results.snippet: Directly serves angle 1 (developmental curve). Documents that comprehension of time-compressed/degraded speech keeps maturing through childhood and adolescence, with right-ear performance reaching adult-like levels by ~age 10 and left-ear by ~age 13-14, reflecting maturation of the central auditory nervous system. Provides the lower-lifespan bracket and the age at which fast-speech ability matures toward young-adult levels.

results.title: Age-related hearing loss accelerates the decline in fast speech comprehension and the decompensation of cortical network connections (PMC)
results.snippet: Addresses mechanisms (angle 3) and steepness of aging decline (angle 2). Shows age-related hearing loss accelerates the loss of fast-speech comprehension and is accompanied by cortical-network decompensation, linking peripheral loss to central/cognitive reorganization rather than treating them as independent. Useful counterpoint to PMC6364265 on whether audibility matters, and adds neural-plasticity/connectivity evidence relevant to the plasticity angle.

results.title: The relationship between the intelligibility of time-compressed speech and speech in noise in young and elderly listeners (Versfeld & Dreschler; PubMed)
results.snippet: Provides young-vs-elderly threshold comparisons and ties time-compression intelligibility to speech-in-noise performance, with discussion of temporal acuity vs audibility (presbycusis) as competing explanations for elderly deficits. Supplies the speech-reception-threshold framing (compression ratio thresholds) that underpins the ~11-16 syllables/sec figures and helps flag where peripheral hearing loss is/ isn't controlled.

results.title: Time-Compressed Speech Identification Is Predicted by Auditory Neural Processing, Perceptuomotor Speed, and Executive Functioning in Younger and Older Listeners (Ear & Hearing / Trends in Hearing)
results.snippet: Directly answers angles 2 and 3. Audibility tightly controlled (all listeners ≤25 dB HL, 250-3000 Hz), so the age penalty is NOT driven by peripheral hearing. Quantifies the steepening decline by compression rate: at 40% compression no age difference (95.5% young vs 92.7% old, p=.11), at 50% old significantly worse (86.8% vs 79.5%, r=.357), at 60% the gap is largest (74.0% vs 53.7%, r=.563). Crucially, mediation analysis shows perceptuomotor PROCESSING SPEED mediates the age effect (age becomes nonsignificant once speed is in the model); P1 latency (auditory neural processing), perceptuomotor speed, and executive function each independently predict performance. This is the highest-signal source for separating central/cognitive from peripheral factors.

results.title: Auditory Temporal Processing and Aging: Implications for Speech Understanding of Older People (Gordon-Salant et al.)
results.snippet: Comprehensive review covering the mechanism dissociation (angle 3) and trainability/blindness modulation (angle 5). States older listeners struggle with time-compressed speech (30-60% compression) 'independent of the effects of hearing loss'; on most temporal tasks 'hearing loss effects were not observed,' isolating central aging. Key audibility-control evidence: young listeners noise-masked to MATCH older listeners' thresholds still outperform older normal-hearing listeners, proving a central deficit beyond audibility. Age effects are steepest at the fastest rates (e.g., 100 ms inter-onset intervals). Also reports older BLIND listeners performing like young normal-hearing listeners on rapid speech, with r=.82-.88 between performance and hours of fast-audio listening, supporting plasticity/trainability.

results.title: Recognition of Time-Compressed Speech Does Not Predict Recognition of Natural Fast-Rate Speech by Older Listeners
results.snippet: Important caveat for the original question's ecological framing. Audibility controlled (≤25 dB HL, 250-4000 Hz). Both age groups recognize artificially time-compressed (TC) speech far better than naturally-fast speech (40% compression / 0.6 ratio tested), and older listeners are disproportionately hurt by natural fast speech, especially in noise. Implication: lab TC-speech thresholds likely OVERESTIMATE real-world fast-talker comprehension and the gap is larger for older adults — relevant when extrapolating the reference listener's ~13 syll/s TC audiobook ability to natural speech.

results.title: The Recognition of Time-Compressed Speech as a Function of Age in Listeners With Cochlear Implants or Normal Hearing (Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience, 2022)
results.snippet: Most directly addresses the continuous aging curve (angle 2). Examines TC-speech recognition as an explicit function of age in normal-hearing vs cochlear-implant listeners, letting peripheral input (CI) be partly dissociated from central age effects. Open-access Frontiers mirror (the PMC copy was CAPTCHA-blocked). Reports a monotonic age-related decline in TC-speech recognition and an age-by-compression interaction, reinforcing that the deficit grows with both age and compression severity.

results.title: The Intelligibility of Time-Compressed Speech Is Correlated With the Ability to Listen in Modulated Noise
results.snippet: Supports the mechanism angle (angle 3) by linking TC-speech intelligibility to temporal-envelope/dip-listening ability rather than pure audibility, consistent with a central temporal-processing account. Useful secondary evidence that the limiting factor in fast-speech comprehension is temporal processing capacity, not just peripheral sensitivity.

results.title: Temporal Envelope Changes of Compression and Speech Rate: Combined Effects on Recognition for Older Adults (JSLHR, Gordon-Salant & Fitzgibbons style)
results.snippet: Adds older-adult-specific quantification of how compression and speech-rate (temporal envelope) changes combine to degrade recognition. Relevant to the steepness/threshold question (angle 2) and to disentangling the temporal-envelope mechanism, with older adults showing greater susceptibility to faster rates than younger listeners.

results.title: Ongoing maturation in the time-compressed speech test (Aslan et al.)
results.snippet: The single most on-target study for the developmental angle. Directly compares children (8-10 yr, mean 9) vs. young adults (16-24 yr, mean 21) on a 60%-compression time-compressed speech test. Young adults significantly outperform children (p<0.001) on both monosyllables (~40.5% vs ~43.8%) and disyllables (~40% vs ~46.7%). Age is a strong predictor for disyllables (r=0.76-0.80; age explains 58-64% of variance) but only moderate for monosyllables (r=0.49-0.54; 21-24% of variance). Crucially supports the mechanism question: the larger adult advantage on disyllables shows that mature performance is driven by top-down/cognitive, lexical-semantic processing (not just sensory encoding), and that this top-down contribution is still immature in children. Establishes that time-compressed speech ability keeps maturing from childhood into early adulthood, though it does not pin a single age threshold.

results.title: Don't speak too fast! Processing of fast-rate speech in children with specific language impairment (Guiraud et al.)
results.snippet: Provides rare quantitative speech-RATE figures in absolute syllables/second, directly comparable to the 13 syll/sec adult benchmark. Tested French children (8-13 yr) at normal (6.76 syll/sec) vs fast (9.15 syll/sec; 1.35x speed-up) rates. Reports that typically-developing children aged 8-9 already adapt to accelerated speech much like adults, needing ~18 sentences of exposure for natural fast speech vs ~6-12 sentences for time-compressed speech. Shows the adaptation skill is substantially in place by ages 8-9, while children with SLI lag, isolating a rhythm/temporal-tracking component of the developmental curve.

results.title: Adult-like processing of time-compressed speech by newborns: A NIRS study (Issard & Gervain)
results.snippet: Anchors the earliest end of the developmental curve. Using NIRS, newborns discriminate normal (100%), moderately compressed (60%), and highly compressed (30%) speech, and their cortical responses mirror adults: canonical hemodynamic response to normal/moderate compression but an inverted response to extreme (30%) compression. Authors conclude adaptation to moderately compressed speech 'requires little or no experience with speech' and occurs at an auditory rather than abstract-linguistic level. Important nuance for the developmental story: the basic peripheral/auditory capacity to handle moderate compression is present at birth, so the protracted childhood-to-adulthood gains seen in older-child studies must reflect maturing central/cognitive (top-down) processing rather than the basic temporal-decoding mechanism.

results.title: Maturation of speech perception in noise abilities during adolescence (Int. J. Pediatric Otorhinolaryngology)
results.snippet: Helps bracket WHEN central auditory speech-in-degraded-conditions processing reaches adult-like levels, the key question for the developmental angle. Reports ear-asymmetric maturation: right-ear speech-in-noise reaches adult scores by ~10 years, but left-ear performance only becomes adult-like by ~13 years, implying central auditory maturation continues into early-mid adolescence. Adjacent task (speech-in-noise rather than pure time-compression) but commonly co-administered and supports the claim that degraded-speech processing matures through the early teens.

results.title: Development of central auditory processes in Polish children and adolescents aged 7-16 years (Current Psychology)
results.snippet: Maps the developmental trajectory of central auditory tasks including time-compressed speech across a wide 7-16 year range. Finds 7- and 8-year-olds perform worse than older groups specifically on dichotic listening and time-compressed speech, with continued improvement through adolescence. Useful for placing the age band where time-compressed speech processing is still immature and quantifying the gradual rise toward adult performance, reinforcing that maturation extends into the teens.

results.title: Effect of age on time-compressed speech perception and speech perception in noise in normal-hearing individuals (Journal of Hearing Science)
results.snippet: Examines time-compressed speech across age in normal-hearing listeners, useful for connecting the developmental rise to the later aging decline within a single normal-hearing framework (helps separate central/cognitive effects from peripheral hearing loss since participants are normal-hearing). Provides comparative data on how time-compressed speech and speech-in-noise track with age, relevant for situating a healthy adult's ~13 syll/sec performance relative to the developmental peak.

results.title: Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans (Dietrich et al., 2013, Front. Hum. Neurosci.)
results.snippet: The single most on-point paper for the age-of-onset / plasticity angle. Late-blind adults (mean age 34.3 yr) trained on ultra-fast speech: 4 of 6 improved by ~70 percentage points (mean +71%) on 18 syl/s material that started below 14% correct, reaching >80% at 8 and 13 syl/s. Crucially shows ONSET and RESIDUAL VISION gate visual-cortex plasticity: only the subject with NO residual vision recruited right V1 after training; signal change correlated negatively with acuity (rho -0.77 to -0.83), with a threshold near 10-20% acuity and optimal V1 use needing acuity <2%. Demonstrates late-blind individuals can still gain the skill behaviorally, but cortical reorganization (V1 recruitment) is far more constrained than in early/congenital blindness. Directly relates to the 13 syl/s reference listener.

results.title: Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – an fMRI study (Dietrich et al., 2013, BMC Neuroscience 14:74)
results.snippet: Establishes the early- vs late-blind divergence the question asks about. Reports blind experts comprehend up to ~22 syl/s vs ~8 syl/s ceiling in sighted; ultra-fast skill covaries with right-V1 (plus fusiform gyrus and bilateral pulvinar) activation. Both high-performing early-blind subjects showed bilateral occipital responses, whereas only one of the late-blind did (most late-blind were right-lateralized), indicating earlier onset of blindness permits more extensive, bilateral visual-cortex recruitment. Provides the syllable-rate ceilings to benchmark the 13 syl/s listener and shows V1 has a causal-style role in speed-listening.

results.title: Age, Hearing, and the Perceptual Learning of Rapid Speech (Manheim, Lavie & Banai, 2018, Ear & Hearing)
results.snippet: Best quantitative source on TRAINABILITY across age with hearing controlled. Baselines equated (~39-44% correct) using different compression per group (young 30% of natural duration, older-normal-hearing 35-40%, older-hearing-impaired 40%), isolating central from peripheral factors. Early rapid learning DECLINED with age (Kruskal-Wallis chi2(2)=24.13, p<.001): young adults median +30% over 20 sentences vs weaker, often non-significant gains in older groups; ONH vs OHI also differed, so hearing loss adds penalty beyond age. Older adults still improve but less, and transfer to novel time-compressed sentences occurred ONLY in young adults — older listeners trained but failed to generalize. Quantifies how much an older adult can realistically gain.

results.title: Improving Older Adults' Understanding of Challenging Speech: Auditory training, rapid adaptation and perceptual learning (Banai & Lavner, 2021, Hearing Research review)
results.snippet: Synthesis tying together the trainability picture for the question's angle 5. When starting accuracy is equated, older adults can adapt to time-compressed/noise-vocoded speech at a rate and magnitude comparable to young adults, BUT maintenance and transfer of learning decline with age. Concludes older adults with good rapid perceptual learning can partially OFFSET the combined effects of age and hearing loss on perception of fast conversational-rate speech — the practical 'can older adults still train?' answer, with the caveat that gains are narrower and less generalizable.

results.title: Auditory Cognitive Training Improves Brain Plasticity in Healthy Older Adults: Evidence From a Randomized Controlled Trial (2022, Front. Aging Neurosci.)
results.snippet: RCT evidence that the aged brain retains trainable plasticity relevant to fast/temporal auditory processing. After just 4 weeks of auditory-cognitive training, healthy older adults showed improved auditory measures plus increased regional gray-matter volume and functional connectivity in the left temporal pole. Supports the mechanism that speed-listening gains in older adults reflect genuine central plasticity (not only peripheral compensation), complementing the behavioral training studies.

results.title: Dissociations in perceptual learning revealed by adult age differences in adaptation to time-compressed speech (Peelle & Wingfield, 2005, JEP:HPP)
results.snippet: Foundational study dissociating learning components by age specifically for time-compressed speech. Shows older adults can rapidly adapt to time-compressed speech at rates similar to young adults for the trained materials, but exhibit reduced generalization/transfer across compression rates — establishing the early-but-fragile learning pattern later replicated under hearing-controlled conditions. Useful older-vs-young contrast for the trainability angle.

claims.claim: Older adults (60-69 years) were more negatively affected by speech compression rate and discard interval length than middle-aged adults (42-54 years), demonstrating a measurable age-related decline in time-compressed speech comprehension between middle and older age.
claims.quote: The older adults were affected more by CR and DIL values than middle-aged adults.

claims.claim: Comprehension of time-compressed speech deteriorates as compression rate increases, with the study testing compression rates of 30%, 45%, and 60% (and discard intervals of 35-155 ms); higher compression produced worse comprehension in all subjects.
claims.quote: In all cases, performance of the subjects deteriorated with increasing CR and DIL.

claims.claim: Time-compressed speech can serve as a clinical tool to detect central/non-peripheral auditory processing deficits, implying the age-related decline reflects central rather than purely peripheral (presbycusis) factors.
claims.quote: time-compressed speech may be an effective signal in clinical assessment of adults whose auditory complaints are not explained by their peripheral hearing losses

claims.claim: Sentence/speech complexity affected younger and older groups equally, indicating that the age penalty for time-compressed speech is driven by compression rate rather than interacting with linguistic complexity in this 42-69 age range.
claims.quote: The difference in sentence complexity between the two speech materials affected both groups equally.

claims.claim: Older adults (56-82) identified time-compressed speech significantly worse than younger adults (19-30) at high compression rates: at 60% compression older listeners scored 0.537 vs 0.740 for younger (t(49)=4.859, p<0.001), and at 50% compression 0.795 vs 0.868 (t(49)=2.725, p=0.009), while at 40% there was no age difference.
claims.quote: Older listeners had more difficulty accurately identifying 50 and 60 % time-compressed speech ... Compared to younger adults, the identification scores of older adults were significantly lower ... no age-group difference was found for 40 % time-compressed speech

claims.claim: The age penalty on time-compressed speech identification is NOT explained by peripheral hearing/audibility: all participants had normal audiometric thresholds (≤25 dB HL, 250-3000 Hz), and adding audiometric thresholds did not improve the model (chi-square(1)=0.130, p=0.718; threshold predictor t(48)=0.350, p=0.728).
claims.quote: audiometric thresholds do not mediate the relationship between age and time-compressed speech identification

claims.claim: The age-related decline in time-compressed speech identification was statistically mediated by perceptuomotor (cognitive/perceptual) processing speed: adding perceptuomotor speed to the model rendered the age-group effect nonsignificant, indicating central processing speed rather than age per se drives the deficit.
claims.quote: the effect of age group...became a nonsignificant predictor in model 2a. The addition of perceptuomotor processing speed in model 2a mediated the effect of age group

claims.claim: Three independent factors predicted time-compressed speech identification: auditory neural processing (longer P1 peak latency = worse, beta=-0.181, p=0.004), faster perceptuomotor processing speed (beta=0.242, p=0.004), and better executive functioning (beta=0.132, p=0.031), all remaining significant simultaneously.
claims.quote: time-compressed speech identification is subject to both low-level peripheral processing and higher-level perceptuomotor processing speed and executive functioning ability

claims.claim: Slower central auditory neural synchrony, indexed by P1 latency, predicts poorer fast-speech identification, linking the age effect to neural temporal processing (myelination/synchronous firing) rather than just audibility.
claims.quote: Shorter click-induced P1 latencies, resulting from better myelinization and more synchronous firing of neurons along the auditory path, may facilitate time-compressed speech identification

claims.claim: Time-compressed speech recognition is at ceiling for younger normal-hearing listeners at 0% and 20% compression but declines starting at 40% compression; the four compression levels corresponded to approximately 3.7, 4.6, 6.4, and 10 syllables/second (0%, 20%, 40%, 60% compression respectively).
claims.quote: Performance was at ceiling for unprocessed speech in the 0 and 20% time-compressed conditions and declined beginning at 40% time compression.

claims.claim: Age-related deficits in recognizing time-compressed speech appear before age 65: middle-aged (50s-60s) and older (65+) groups showed similar deficits relative to younger listeners, with the largest declines at 40-60% compression.
claims.quote: The older and middle age groups showed similar performances, regardless of the mode of listening...indicating that potential age-related differences in central auditory processing may affect performance by adults prior to 65 years of age.

claims.claim: The age penalty for time-compressed speech persists after controlling for peripheral hearing, because cochlear-implant users (who bypass much peripheral cochlear pathology) showed the same age-related decline as normal-hearing listeners; thus the deficit is attributed primarily to central auditory processing rather than presbycusis.
claims.quote: the deficits in speech recognition of older listeners for time-compressed speech may be primarily affected by age-related declines in central auditory processing rather than solely related to peripheral age-related hearing loss.

claims.claim: General cognitive processing speed did NOT predict recognition of time-compressed speech, arguing against a general processing-speed mechanism for the age effect.
claims.quote: cognitive processing speed did not predict recognition of time-compressed speech

claims.claim: There was no significant listener-group (CI vs NH) by age-group interaction (all p's > 0.05), indicating age-related differences in time-compressed speech recognition are similar in both CI and NH listeners; quantitatively, older NH listeners at 60% compression had log-odds estimate -0.96 (z = -8.27, p < 0.001) relative to younger.
claims.quote: There were no significant listener group × age group interactions (all p's > 0.05). This lack of an interaction between listener group and age group suggests that the age-related differences in recognition of time-compressed speech...may be similar in both CI and NH listener groups.

claims.claim: In a time-compressed speech test at 60% compression, young adults (16-24 yrs, mean 21) significantly outperformed children (8-10 yrs, mean 9) on both monosyllables and disyllables (all p<0.001), with adults scoring ~46.70 vs ~39.67 on disyllables (right ear). This demonstrates that the ability to comprehend time-compressed speech is still maturing during childhood/adolescence and is higher in young adults.
claims.quote: Young adults significantly outperformed children in both monosyllables and disyllables (all p<0.001); Children disyllables right ear: mean 39.67 (SD 2.50); Adults disyllables right ear: mean 46.70 (SD 2.02)

claims.claim: Age was a strong predictor of performance on the disyllable (more cognitively demanding) modality and only a moderate predictor on the monosyllable modality, with age explaining 64.4% / 57.7% of variance for disyllables versus only 23.6% / 21.4% for monosyllables. The maturational age effect is larger for stimuli requiring more top-down cognitive inference.
claims.quote: Age was a strong predictor of TCST performance in the disyllable modality, and a moderate correlation was observed in the monosyllable modality. Linear regression variance explained by age: Disyllables: 64.4% (right ear), 57.7% (left ear); Monosyllables: 23.6% (right ear), 21.4% (left ear)

claims.claim: All 60 participants had normal peripheral hearing and no neurological, psychiatric, cognitive or central auditory processing deficits, so the observed age/maturation effect on time-compressed speech is not attributable to peripheral hearing loss (audibility was controlled).
claims.quote: The 60 subjects had normal peripheral hearing and good school performance and were without neurological, psychiatric, cognitive or central auditory processing deficits.

claims.claim: The time-compressed speech test probes auditory closure, but as more of the acoustic signal is removed the task shifts toward a cognitive/top-down process, implicating central and cognitive mechanisms rather than peripheral hearing in comprehending degraded fast speech.
claims.quote: When a large proportion of the acoustic signal is missing, the test becomes cognitive because the individual must use top-down skills to infer the complete message.

claims.claim: This study only examined development through young adulthood (max age 24) and did not specify an age at which performance plateaus, nor did it test older adults; thus it provides no data on aging-related decline of time-compressed speech comprehension.
claims.quote: The paper does not specify an age at which performance stabilizes. This study does not examine older adults or age-related decline beyond young adulthood. The research addresses only developmental maturation through young adulthood (maximum age 24 years).

claims.claim: Even after correcting/controlling for hearing loss, elderly normal-hearing listeners comprehend time-compressed (and noisy) speech worse than young normal-hearing listeners, indicating a central (non-audibility) age penalty.
claims.quote: All tests indicate that, even after correction for the hearing loss, elderly normal-hearing subjects perform worse than young normal-hearing subjects.

claims.claim: The time-compression threshold (TCT) is operationalized as the speech rate, expressed in syllables per second, at which 50% of sentences are correctly perceived, making syllables/second the relevant unit for age-band comparisons.
claims.quote: In analogy to the SRT, the time-compression threshold (or TCT) then is defined as the speech rate (expressed in syllables per second) for which half of the sentences are correctly perceived.

claims.claim: Age-related difficulty understanding accelerated/adverse speech is attributable partly to presbycusis (audibility) but also to non-peripheral factors such as temporal acuity, which time-compressed speech is used to probe.
claims.quote: This may be partly due to the differences in hearing sensitivity (presbycusis), hence audibility, but other factors, such as temporal acuity, may also play a significant role.

claims.claim: Time-compression threshold performance is highly correlated with speech reception in fluctuating noise, suggesting a shared temporal-processing mechanism underlies both tasks.
claims.quote: The results show that the SRT in fluctuating noise and the TCT are highly correlated.

claims.claim: The study design directly disentangles audibility from central factors by crossing age (young vs elderly) with hearing status (normal-hearing vs hearing-impaired) in four subject groups.
claims.quote: In experiment II, four groups of subjects (young and elderly normal-hearing and hearing-impaired subjects) participated, and the SRT's in stationary and fluctuating speech-shaped noise were determined, as well as the TCT.

results.title: Verbal Working Memory in Older Adults: The Roles of Phonological Capacities and Processing Speed (J Speech Lang Hear Res, 2017)
results.snippet: Most direct hit on the cognitive-mediator question. Cleanly dissociates the two candidate mechanisms: PROCESSING SPEED dominates the BETWEEN-age-group effect, while PHONOLOGICAL-LOOP capacity is preserved with age and only predicts WITHIN-older-group variance. Age effect on serial recall accuracy was large (eta-squared = .206 for 8-item, .281 for 6-item lists); covarying response time (processing speed) substantially REDUCED the age main effect (down to eta-squared = .111 / .089). Phonological measures (final-consonant choice, backwards-words, lexical decision) showed NO age-group differences; phonological processing predicted individual recall variance among older adults (standardized beta = .399-.449). Bottom line: rapid/speeded-speech difficulty in aging reflects processing-speed limits, not degraded phonological representations -- exactly the disentangling the question asks for.

results.title: Speech recognition in noise across the life span with cognition and hearing sensitivity as mediators of age effects (Scientific Reports, 2025)
results.snippet: Recent large lifespan mediation study (N=357, ages 7-90) that formally tests auditory working memory, processing speed, AND hearing sensitivity as parallel mediators of the age effect -- directly addressing whether the cognitive penalty persists after accounting for audibility. Identifies processing speed and working memory as cognitive mediators alongside peripheral hearing, letting the reader weigh which arm dominates across the full age range. Best single source for quantifying how much of the age effect survives audibility controls.

results.title: Age Differences in Text Processing: The Role of Working Memory, Inhibition, and Processing Speed (J Gerontol B, 2011)
results.snippet: Provides the mediation ARCHITECTURE that ties processing speed and working memory together. SEM on younger (M=23) vs older (M=70) adults shows a cascade: working memory is the PROXIMAL mediator of the age->comprehension link, while processing speed (and inhibition) act UPSTREAM by accounting for the age-related variance in working memory. Reconciles the two mechanisms -- speed is the distal driver, WM the bottleneck -- which is essential for interpreting which factor 'dominates' the age effect on rapid/language processing.

results.title: Effects of working memory capacity on understanding rate-altered speech (Aging, Neuropsychology, and Cognition, 1997)
results.snippet: Classic Wingfield-tradition study explicitly linking WORKING-MEMORY CAPACITY to comprehension of RATE-ALTERED (time-compressed and time-expanded) speech in young vs older adults. Shows recall is significantly worse at 60% time-compression than at normal rate, and that restoring time fails to lift older adults back to baseline -- evidence that WM capacity gates how much speed reduction helps. Directly on-topic for the 13 syllables/second time-compressed-audiobook framing.

results.title: Effects of age, acoustic challenge, and verbal working memory on recall of narrative speech (PMC, 2016)
results.snippet: Tests verbal working memory as a moderator of age and acoustic-challenge effects on recall of connected/narrative speech (the audiobook-like case). Useful for the individual-differences arm: older listeners with better short-term memory (backward digit span) adapted faster to degraded speech, and slower speech rates can paradoxically tax WM by lengthening retention demands -- nuancing the 'slower is always easier' assumption relevant to a fast-listening adult.

results.title: The recognition of time-compressed speech as a function of age in listeners with cochlear implants or normal hearing (PMC, 2022)
results.snippet: Provides a useful counterpoint/boundary condition for the cognitive-mediator claim: the age-related deficit for time-compressed speech was robust, but a measure of cognitive PROCESSING SPEED did NOT predict the magnitude of the time-compression effect in this sample. Important for flagging that the processing-speed-dominance finding is not universal across paradigms, and that peripheral/central auditory factors must be weighed alongside cognition.

claims.claim: Younger adults (18-30 years) have an average time-compression threshold (TCT) for fast speech of 15.6 words/second, the highest of the groups tested, providing a young-adult benchmark for accelerated speech comprehension.
claims.quote: The average TCT in younger individuals was 15.6 words/s.

claims.claim: Older adults (over 60) comprehend time-compressed speech significantly worse than younger adults, and those with presbycusis decline even more than older adults with normal hearing, demonstrating both an aging effect and an additive hearing-loss effect on fast-speech comprehension.
claims.quote: presbycusis-younger: t = 16.13, P < 0.001; normal older-younger: t = 10.66, P < 0.001

claims.claim: The age penalty in fast-speech comprehension partly persists independent of peripheral hearing loss: older adults with normal hearing thresholds (<=25 dB HL) still showed deficits, with frontal-lobe changes attributed to increased auditory/listening effort rather than audibility loss.
claims.quote: the increased activity in the frontal lobe observed in older individuals appears to be related to increased auditory effort

claims.claim: Normal aging recruits compensatory central resources, shown as enhanced functional connectivity across multiple brain networks (including the default mode network), whereas age-related hearing loss causes these compensatory connections to break down (decompensation), implicating central rather than purely peripheral mechanisms in the decline.
claims.quote: those with age-related hearing loss exhibit decompensation of network connections involving multisensory integration

claims.claim: Age-related hearing loss specifically accelerates the decline in fast-speech comprehension beyond the effect of normal aging, particularly under competing-speech conditions.
claims.quote: hearing loss accelerates the decline in speech comprehension, especially in speech competition situations

claims.claim: Older normal-hearing listeners (ages 66-76) are more detrimentally affected by fast speech than young normal-hearing listeners (ages 19-22), with younger listeners significantly outperforming older listeners on time-compressed speech (p=0.012) and natural-fast speech (p=0.005) in noise, while showing no difference on normal-rate speech (p=0.52). This age penalty persists despite both groups having normal hearing sensitivity (pure-tone thresholds <=25 dB HL).
claims.quote: the older listeners were more detrimentally affected by the fast speech rate, especially naturally fast speech, compared to the younger listeners

claims.claim: The age-related deficit in fast-speech recognition was attributed to central/cognitive factors (processing speed, attention, working memory) rather than peripheral audibility, since all participants had normal hearing thresholds <=25 dB HL at 250-4000 Hz, demonstrating the age penalty after controlling for audibility.
claims.quote: All participants were native speakers of American English who had normal hearing sensitivity, as defined by pure-tone air conduction thresholds <=25 dB HL

claims.claim: Recognition of time-compressed speech consistently overestimates (is higher than) recognition of natural fast-rate speech for both younger and older listeners, meaning time-compressed-speech performance does not reliably predict natural fast-speech comprehension; in noise the correlation between the two was only modest (HP sentences r=0.651, AP sentences r=0.569).
claims.quote: Recognition of TC materials was consistently higher than recognition of natural-fast speech materials for both listener groups, in both contexts, and in both environmental conditions

claims.claim: Older listeners face a greater disadvantage with natural fast speech than would be predicted from their time-compressed-speech recognition performance, indicating laboratory time-compression methods understate real-world aging deficits.
claims.quote: older listeners are at a much greater disadvantage when listening to natural fast speech than would be predicted by recognition performance

claims.claim: The study used 40% time-compression (fast-to-normal ratio = 0.6) for its fast-speech stimuli, and there was a significant rate-by-group interaction in noise (F(2,46)=3.597, p<0.05) showing the older group's selective vulnerability to the faster rate.
claims.quote: the fast sentences approximated 40% time-compression (fast-to-normal ratio = 0.6)

claims.claim: In young normal-hearing adults (mean age ~23), the ability to perceive time-compressed (fast) speech is positively correlated with the ability to derive information from glimpses when listening to speech in modulated noise (partial correlation controlling for unmodulated-noise performance: r=0.41, p=0.008).
claims.quote: significant partial correlation between the ability to perceive speech at fast rates (SRTrate) and speech perception in the modulated noise conditions (SRTmod) for the original (r_pearson = 0.41, p = 0.008)

claims.claim: Young normal-hearing listeners reach a speech reception threshold at a compression ratio of 0.179, corresponding to syllable rates of about 12.3-17.0 syllables/sec (and phoneme rates of 33.5-46.6 Hz, word rates of 8.2-11.4 Hz), providing a young-adult performance baseline for time-compressed speech.
claims.quote: an average phoneme, syllable, and word rate of respectively 33.5–46.6 Hz, 12.3–17.0 Hz, and 8.2–11.4 Hz

claims.claim: Special populations can perceive time-compressed speech at rates up to 22 syllables per second, indicating the neural mechanisms processing fast speech are plastic and can be altered by exposure and training.
claims.quote: it is not uncommon that special populations perceive speech at rates up to 22 syllables per second...indicating that the neural processing mechanisms of time-compressed speech can be altered by, for example, exposure and training

claims.claim: The study deliberately restricted participants to young normal-hearing listeners specifically to exclude age and hearing as confounding factors, so it does not measure aging effects on time-compressed speech and references aging effects only secondhand via prior work (Gordon-Salant et al.).
claims.quote: We selected young normal-hearing listeners so that no confounding factors such as age and hearing could play a role.

claims.claim: Reduced intelligibility of time-compressed speech is attributed to a cortical-mechanism mismatch: as speech rate increases, spectro-temporal modulations within the cortical integration window no longer match the internal representations used to process normal-rate speech.
claims.quote: when the speech rate increases, the spectro-temporal modulations within a specific integration window do not correspond anymore to the internal representations used to process speech at normal rates, and hence speech intelligibility is reduced

claims.claim: Older listeners experience considerable difficulty understanding speech at faster-than-normal (time-compressed) rates independently of peripheral hearing loss; the effects of age and hearing loss operate separately and are additive, so the age penalty persists after controlling for audibility.
claims.quote: older listeners experience considerable difficulty understanding speech presented at faster-than-normal rates, independent of the effects of hearing loss ... effects of hearing loss and age operate independently, and therefore may become additive

claims.claim: The age-related deficit on time-compressed speech is demonstrated across compression ratios of 30, 40, 50, and 60%, with older listeners performing worse than younger adults; significant main effects of age, hearing loss, and time-compression ratio were observed.
claims.quote: time-compressed by 30, 40, 50, and 60% ... Significant main effects of age, hearing loss, and time compression ratio were observed

claims.claim: Experienced older blind listeners comprehend rapid/time-compressed speech as well as younger sighted adults and significantly better than older sighted adults, indicating the rapid-speech difficulty of older adults is trainable rather than an inherent consequence of aging.
claims.quote: the difficulty older listeners experience in understanding rapid speech is not inherent to the aging process, but rather, can be ameliorated through training and experience with speech presented at faster-than-normal rates

claims.claim: Performance on time-compressed speech correlates strongly (r = .82 in quiet, .88 in noise) with the number of hours blind listeners spent listening to faster-than-normal-rate speech materials (e.g., books on tape), quantifying the dose-response of speed-listening training.
claims.quote: The r values were .82 and .88 for time-compressed speech in quiet and in noise, respectively

claims.claim: The age effect on rapid speech is attributed to multiple central/cognitive mechanisms rather than peripheral hearing alone: age-related deterioration of central auditory neural temporal coding, declining working-memory capacity, and slowed perceptual processing speed; the paper treats temporal processing as the unifying framework but does not isolate a single dominant factor.
claims.quote: age-related deterioration of neural cells likely affects the ability to code the onsets of signals and successive onsets of a sequence of changing signals, such as occurs in speech

claims.claim: Newborns (mean age 2.34 days, range 1-4 days) process moderately time-compressed speech (60% of normal duration) with the same canonical hemodynamic brain response as normal-rate speech, with no significant channel-by-channel difference between the two, indicating adult-like adaptation to moderate compression is present at birth.
claims.quote: responses to normal and moderately compressed speech are similar, showing a canonical hemodynamic response in the left temporoparietal, right frontal and right temporal cortex, while responses to highly compressed speech are inverted

claims.claim: The neural ability to adapt to time-compressed speech does not require postnatal speech experience and operates at an auditory rather than abstract linguistic level, implying the basic machinery for fast/time-compressed speech processing is innate and present from birth.
claims.quote: adaptation to time-compressed speech requires little or no experience with speech, and happens at an auditory, and not at a more abstract linguistic level

claims.claim: Newborn brains distinguish normal speech from highly compressed speech (30% of normal duration) but not from moderately compressed speech (60%), paralleling the adult intelligibility boundary where 60% compression remains intelligible and 30% does not.
claims.quote: Pair-wise comparisons showed significant differences between normal and 30% compressed speech (p = 0.001 to p = 0.007) but not between normal and 60% compressed speech

claims.claim: Highly compressed speech (30% duration) produced significant decreases in oxyhemoglobin in specific cortical channels in newborns, indicating a qualitatively different (inverted) neural response at extreme compression rates that exceeds processing capacity.
claims.quote: Significant decreases in oxyhemoglobin in channels 10, 12, 15, and 22 (p < 0.001 to p = 0.025)

claims.claim: Time-compressed speech processing in newborns engages a left temporoparietal, right frontal, and right temporal network, with temporal/temporo-parietal regions more active for compressed conditions, suggesting prosodic processing is recruited for fast speech adaptation.
claims.quote: higher activation in the temporal/temporo-parietal regions for both compression rates

claims.claim: Untrained/normal listeners comprehend speech at roughly 8 syllables/second, whereas trained blind listeners can comprehend ultra-fast speech up to about 22 syllables/second — placing the research subject's reported ~13 syl/s between these benchmarks.
claims.quote: ca. 8 syl/s ... up to about 22 syllables (syl) per seconds (s)

claims.claim: Late-blind adults can be trained (over ~6 months) to dramatically improve ultra-fast speech comprehension: most subjects went from below 14% correct at 18 syllables/second before training to a mean improvement of ~71 percentage points after training, demonstrating substantial adult plasticity for speed-listening.
claims.quote: Prior to the training procedure, none of the subjects was able to understand ultra-fast speech at a level of 18 syl/s. More specifically, the percentage of correctly reproduced syllables fell below 14% in all instances. ... Four subjects (nos. 147, 150, 144, and 142) showed an improved behavioral performance of up to ca. 70% points (mean = 71%, SE = 2.74%)

claims.claim: Age of blindness onset / degree of residual vision modulates visual-cortex (V1) recruitment for ultra-fast speech: only subjects with very low visual acuity showed training-induced activation of the central-visual system, and residual vision correlated negatively with V1 signal change (ρ = −0.83, p < 0.05 after training).
claims.quote: Only subjects with very low visual acuity displayed training-induced hemodynamic activation of the central-visual system. ... a significant negative correlation between residual vision and the percent signal change during the forward ultra-fast speech condition (vs. baseline) could be detected before (ρ = −0.77, p < 0.05) and after the training sessions (ρ = −0.83, p < 0.05)

claims.claim: The trainable plasticity for ultra-fast speech was demonstrated in middle-aged adults (mean age 34.3 years, SD 11.99) with blindness onset ranging from age 3 to 45, indicating speed-listening gains are achievable well beyond childhood/adolescence.
claims.quote: mean age = 34.3 years, SD = 11.99

claims.claim: Functional reorganization of the visual cortex underpins ultra-fast speech comprehension specifically in totally blind individuals: the only subject with no residual vision showed significant positive V1 activation under forward ultra-fast speech after training.
claims.quote: Only subject no. 147 (no residual vision) showed significantly positive values under the forward ultra-fast speech condition following the training sessions

claims.claim: Speech perception in noise (SPIN) scores in the right ear are mature and comparable to adults by age 10 years, while left-ear SPIN scores become adult-like only between 13 and 14 years of age.
claims.quote: Results demonstrated that SPIN scores in the right ear were matured by the age of 10 years and were comparable with the right ear SPIN scores in adults. Pairwise comparison using Bonferroni's corrections for multiple comparisons of left ear SPIN scores revealed that SPIN scores in the left ear become adult-like between 13 and 14 years of age.

claims.claim: Speech-in-noise perception improves with age throughout childhood, with the maturation of adult-like performance occurring during adolescence rather than earlier childhood.
claims.quote: Speech perception in noise scores improves with age in children and is crucial in their routine communications, including classroom learning... The present study aimed to assess and understand the maturation pattern of speech perception in noise abilities during adolescence.

claims.claim: Speech perception depends on extraction of spectro-temporal cues (temporal envelope, temporal fine structure, spectral shape), implicating central auditory temporal processing as a key factor in degraded-speech (noise) perception that matures with age.
claims.quote: Speech perception encompasses the perception of spectro-temporal cues. These cues include temporal envelope, temporal fine structure, and spectral shape of the signal. Extraction of these cues is essential for speech perception and, most importantly, for perceiving speech in the presence of noise (SPIN).

claims.claim: The study was conducted on a cross-sectional sample of 146 participants spanning ages 10-15 years plus adults aged 18-19 years, divided into six age groups, providing an empirical age-band design for tracking maturation.
claims.quote: The study included 146 participants who were divided into six crossectional age groups. Participants were in the age range of 10-15 years and adults of 18-19 years. SPIN was assessed for right and left ears for each of these sub-groups.

claims.claim: In typically developing children aged 8-13, age within this range did not significantly predict comprehension performance on fast-rate or time-compressed speech, suggesting the ability is already substantially functional by age 8 (though the study did not stratify a fine developmental trajectory).
claims.quote: The regression analysis including age as a fixed factor did not reveal any significant influence of this variable on performance in the TD and SLI groups.

claims.claim: Typically developing 8-13 year-olds reliably detect semantic incongruities even when speech is accelerated to ~9.15 syllables/second (fast) or time-compressed to 74% of original duration (1.35x speedup), showing children of this age can process speech rates well above the normal ~6.76 syllables/second.
claims.quote: Normal rate: 6.76 syllables/sec (SD 0.57) Fast rate: 9.15 syllables/sec (SD 0.60) Speed-up factor: 0.74 compression ratio (1.35x speedup)

claims.claim: Children with specific language impairment (SLI) comprehend fast and time-compressed speech significantly worse than age-matched TD peers, while normal-rate comprehension does not differ significantly, indicating fast-speech processing is selectively vulnerable to a developmental language disorder.
claims.quote: children with SLI performed significantly worse than their TD age peers when listening to natural fast (p < .030) and time-compressed sentences (p < .001)," but "the difference between the two groups did not reach significance in the normal rate condition (p = .246)

claims.claim: The deficit in fast-speech processing was attributed to impaired rhythmic/oscillatory entrainment (temporal sampling of syllabic rhythm) rather than to general cognitive processing-speed limitations, since response times did not differ between groups.
claims.quote: No significant difference emerged between the two groups regarding response times," contrary to predictions based on processing speed limitations. The authors propose: "poor entrainment to amplitude fluctuations that characterize speech rhythm

claims.claim: Peripheral hearing was addressed only as an exclusion criterion (absence of hearing problems) rather than via reported formal audiometry, so audibility was a screening requirement but no audiometric thresholds were documented to confirm the central/cognitive locus of the effect.
claims.quote: "absence of hearing problem" was specified as an inclusion criterion for children with SLI. However, the document does not explicitly state whether formal audiometry or audiological testing was conducted.

claims.claim: Trained blind listeners can comprehend speech at up to 22 syllables/second, versus ~8 syl/s for untrained sighted subjects; in this study blind subjects achieved a mean 59.7% comprehension of ultra-fast (16 syl/s) speech (range 0-93%) while sighted controls fell below 16% (mean 9.0%), a highly significant difference (T=7.74, p<.001).
claims.quote: repeated exposure to accelerated verbal utterances may enable blind people to understand spoken language at speaking rates of up to 22 syllables (syl) per second... exceeding by far the upper limits of untrained subjects (ca. 8 syl/s)... blind and sighted individuals significantly differed with respect to speech comprehension of ultra-fast test materials (two-sample t-test; T = 7.74, p < .001)

claims.claim: Ultra-fast speech comprehension ability in blind listeners did NOT correlate significantly with age of onset of vision loss or duration of blindness; early-blind (n=3) vs late-blind (n=11) groups were too small for meaningful statistical comparison.
claims.quote: The enhanced perceptual skills of blind listeners did not show a significant correlation with either the onset of vision loss (r = -.347, p = .224) or with the duration of blindness

claims.claim: Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engaged right primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus, and bilateral pulvinar, with V1 and FG activation positively correlated with individual comprehension skill (V1: r=.533, p<.05; FG: r=.650, p<.05).
claims.quote: blind people highly proficient in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant hemodynamic activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG), and bilateral pulvinar (Pv)... BOLD signal changes within these two areas showed a positive correlation with individual ultra-fast speech perception skills

claims.claim: The visual-cortex recruitment underlying ultra-fast speech in blind subjects reflects task-dependent cross-modal functional plasticity rather than major structural rewiring, though early-blind subjects showed more extensive and bilateral occipital responses than late-blind subjects.
claims.quote: ultra-fast speech perception does not appear to depend upon major rewiring of visual cortex...rather, this perceptual capability seems associated with task-dependent cross-modal functional plasticity

claims.claim: At moderately fast speech (8 syl/s) blind and sighted groups performed comparably (blind mean 84.1%, sighted 80.8%; T=0.74, p=.470), so the blind advantage is specific to ultra-fast rates rather than a general speech-comprehension difference.
claims.quote: Moderately fast utterances (8 syl/s) yielded comparable results in both subject groups... sighted controls: mean = 80.8%, SD ± 11.8%; blind listeners: mean = 84.1%, SD ± 11.0%... two-sample t-test; T = 0.74, p = .470

claims.claim: In normal-hearing native Kannada speakers aged 15-50, young adults significantly outperformed middle-aged adults on both time-compressed speech (TCST) and speech-in-noise (SPIN) tasks, indicating age-related decline in adverse-listening speech comprehension occurs even within mid-adulthood and even without peripheral hearing loss.
claims.quote: Young adults performed better than middle-aged adults in both the TCST and SPIN tests

claims.claim: The study attributes the age-related decline in time-compressed speech and speech-in-noise comprehension to deterioration of CENTRAL auditory processing rather than peripheral hearing, since participants were normal-hearing.
claims.quote: ageing has a deleterious effect on the performance of the central auditory processing system, which is necessary for speech perception under adverse listening conditions

claims.claim: Time-compressed speech comprehension declines monotonically as compression difficulty increases, with the study testing compression ratios of 0%, 40%, 50%, and 60% and performance dropping as difficulty rose.
claims.quote: As difficulty levels rose, performance decreased in both tests

claims.claim: The study sample comprised 29 young and middle-aged adults aged 15 to 50 years, all normal-hearing, isolating age effects from presbycusis but limiting findings to this mid-life range (not older adults in their 60s-80s).
claims.quote: 29 young and middle-aged adults (native Kannada speakers) aged 15 to 50 years

claims.claim: Among older adults with mild-to-moderate hearing loss, speech recognition of low-predictability sentences (including 50% time-compressed sentences) did not differ between a young-old group (62-74 yrs, n=11) and an old-old group (75-88 yrs, n=14); there were no age interactions, indicating no further decline across this older age band.
claims.quote: There was no difference in performance between age groups, or any interactions involving age... For this sample tested, the effect was not age related within the age range tested here.

claims.claim: Time-compressed (rapid-rate) speech is more vulnerable to temporal-envelope distortion than normal-rate speech for older listeners: at the highest envelope-distortion (EDI) value, the reduction in recognition was greater for the 50% time-compressed condition than the normal-rate condition.
claims.quote: There was a significant interaction between speech rate and EDI value; as the EDI value increased, representing higher amounts of temporal envelope distortion, speech recognition was significantly reduced. At the highest EDI value, this reduction was greater for the time-compressed than the normal rate condition.

claims.claim: The age-group comparison was confound-controlled for non-auditory factors: the young-old and old-old groups did not differ in degree of hearing loss, cognition, working memory, or self-reported health, isolating the (null) within-older-adult age effect from those mechanisms.
claims.quote: Two groups of adults with mild to moderate hearing loss were tested: (a) young-old (62-74 years, n=11) and (b) old-old (75-88 years, n=14). The groups did not differ in hearing loss, cognition, working memory, or self-reported health status.

claims.claim: Restoring the original duration to a time-compressed signal did not recover the lost recognition, implying that the recognition deficit was driven by temporal-envelope (central/temporal processing) distortion rather than simply by the faster presentation rate.
claims.quote: When time was restored to the time-compressed signals, speech recognition did not improve... Temporal envelope changes were detrimental to recognition of low-context speech for older listeners once a certain threshold of distortion was reached, particularly for rapid rate speech.

claims.claim: Older listeners appear to use acoustic redundancy as a compensatory mechanism to offset envelope-distortion effects at normal speech rates, a compensation that breaks down for rapid/compressed speech.
claims.quote: The results of the time-restored condition suggested that listeners were using acoustic redundancy to compensate for the negative effects of WDRC distortion in the normal rate condition.

claims.claim: Older adults (mean ages 70-72) showed weaker rapid early learning of time-compressed speech than young adults (mean age 27): 88% of YA improved (median +30%) vs 77% of older normal-hearing and only 61% of older hearing-impaired, with the group difference highly significant (Kruskal-Wallis chi-square(2)=24.13, p<.001) and persisting after baseline audibility was equated.
claims.quote: The magnitude of learning was nevertheless influenced by both age and hearing loss, with mean ranks 95 (YA), 70 (ONH), 51 (OHI). Mann-Whitney contrasts: YA vs. ONH (Z = -3.06, p = .002); ONH vs. OHI (Z = -2.08, p = .037).

claims.claim: Older adults can still improve their time-compressed speech comprehension through training, but their gains do not generalize: young adults transferred learning to untrained time-compressed sentences whereas in both older groups transfer to untrained items was completely absent (age x training interaction F=8.4, p=.005).
claims.quote: Trained YA showed larger gains on repeated time-compressed sentences than ONH and OHI (age effect: F = 8.4, p = .005 for age x training interaction). Transfer to untrained time-compressed sentences: YA transferred; both older groups showed "transfer to untrained items was altogether absent."

claims.claim: The age-related decline in learning rapid speech is dissociable from peripheral hearing loss: after equating baseline audibility, age effects on early learning and transfer persisted independently, while hearing status had no significant effect on training and transfer to natural-fast speech (p=.40 to .96), implicating central/cognitive rather than purely audibility mechanisms.
claims.quote: none of the effects that involved hearing status was significant" for training and transfer to natural-fast speech (p = .40-.96)... Age-related declines in learning may stem from age differences in the use of high- and low-level speech cues

claims.claim: When baseline time-compressed performance was equated across age groups using different compression rates (YA at 30% of natural duration, older normal-hearing at 35-40%, older hearing-impaired at 40%), baseline percent-correct was statistically similar across all three groups (p=.27, range 36-44% correct), demonstrating older adults need less aggressive compression to match young-adult performance.
claims.quote: Baseline performance on time-compressed speech was similar in the three groups" (p = .27), ranging 36-44% correct.

claims.claim: On natural fast speech (166 words/min, not artificially compressed), comprehension declined sharply with age: young adults scored 66+/-13% correct versus 43+/-19% for older normal-hearing and 34+/-15% for older hearing-impaired adults (p<.001), showing a steep age penalty in real-world fast-speech understanding.
claims.quote: for natural-fast speech (166 words/min), older groups performed significantly worse: YA 66+/-13% correct vs. ONH 43+/-19% vs. OHI 34+/-15% (p < .001).

claims.claim: Central auditory temporal-pattern processing (Duration Pattern Test and Frequency Pattern Test) matures gradually through childhood, with 7-year-olds scoring substantially lower than all participants aged 13 and older, indicating that central temporal processing relevant to fast-speech decoding is not adult-like until early adolescence (~13 years).
claims.quote: At the age of 7 there was lower DPT and FPT scores than in all participants ≥13

claims.claim: Dichotic listening (a central auditory process) reaches adult-like performance earlier for the right ear (~age 10) than the left ear (~age 13), with overall dichotic-listening development in Polish children completed by 13 years of age.
claims.quote: outcomes for the right ear became adult-like already at the age of 10 ... the development of dichotic listening in Polish children is completed until 13 years of age

claims.claim: Peripheral hearing was strictly controlled: all participants had normal audiometric thresholds (≤15 dB HL from 250-8000 Hz) and type A tympanograms, so the observed age-related differences reflect central rather than peripheral (presbycusis-type) factors.
claims.quote: All subjects had normal hearing in both ears with pure tones thresholds ≤15 dB HL at the octave frequencies from 250 to 8000 Hz and type A tympanograms

claims.claim: Quantitatively, Frequency Pattern Test scores rose from 52.9% in 7-year-olds to ~78-80% in 13-15-year-olds and 82.6% in adults, and Duration Pattern Test scores rose from 60.5% to 88.3% in adults, showing a steep developmental gain in central temporal processing across childhood.

Gap detection and speech-in-noise comprehension showed no significant age effect across the 7-16 age range, indicating some auditory temporal abilities mature earlier (before age 7) while pattern-recognition and dichotic abilities continue developing into adolescence.
claims.quote: The intergroup differences in the GDT and aSpN scores were not statistically relevant

claims.claim: Cognitive/perceptual processing speed (slowing) is the dominant factor driving age-related decline in verbal working memory, while phonological capacities account for individual variability among older adults rather than the primary age trajectory.
claims.quote: Slowing in processing abilities accounts for the primary trajectory of age-related declines in verbal working memory. However, individual differences in phonological capacities explain variability among individual older adults.

claims.claim: When processing speed (response time) was statistically controlled as a covariate, the age effect on serial-recall accuracy was reduced but not fully eliminated, indicating processing speed mediates but does not exhaust the age penalty.
claims.quote: for eight-item lists, F(1, 40) = 5.01, p = .031, η² = .111; for six-item lists, F(1, 40) = 3.05, p = .089

claims.claim: Older adults (60-80) showed significantly poorer serial-recall accuracy than younger adults (18-32), with effect sizes η² = .206-.281, even though forward and reverse digit spans were equivalent across age groups.
claims.quote: Accuracy on the serial recall task was poorer for older than for younger adults

claims.claim: Peripheral hearing was measured and controlled: pure-tone thresholds from 250-8000 Hz were obtained, an inclusion threshold of better than 45-dB HL at 2000 Hz was required, and no participant used amplification, so the residual age effect on verbal working memory persists after audibility screening.
claims.quote: Auditory thresholds for the octave frequencies between 250 Hz and 8000 Hz were measured, and better-ear pure-tone averages (PTAs) were computed.

claims.claim: Within older adults, both phonological capacity (backwards-words task) and processing speed (response time) independently predicted six-item serial recall, showing phonological-loop and speed contributions are separable.
claims.quote: performance on the backwards words task...explained a significant amount of variance, standardized β = .410, p = .042 and response time, standardized β = −.357, p = .046

result.publishDate: 2016-12 (Journal of Speech, Language, and Hearing Research, 2017 issue; Nittrouer et al.)

claims.claim: In a study comparing younger adults (n=89, M=23.11 years) and older adults (n=102, M=70.50 years), working memory (WM) mediated the influence of age on text processing in both conditions tested.
claims.quote: The best fitting model showed that WM mediated the influence of age on both text processing conditions.

claims.claim: The age-related decline in working memory was itself accounted for by slower processing speed and weaker inhibition, placing processing speed and inhibition upstream of WM in the causal chain of age effects.
claims.quote: Age-related variance in WM was, in turn, accounted for by processing speed and inhibition.

claims.claim: Age differences in text comprehension were larger under more memory-demanding conditions (when the text was absent), indicating older adults rely more heavily on working memory when external support is removed.
claims.quote: Age differences were larger when the text was absent

claims.claim: Older adults show significantly poorer recognition of time-compressed (TC) sentences than younger adults even when peripheral hearing sensitivity is matched, indicating the age penalty persists after controlling for audibility and is driven by central/cognitive factors rather than peripheral hearing loss alone.
claims.quote: Older listeners, with and without hearing loss, demonstrate significantly poorer recognition of TC sentences compared to younger listeners with comparable hearing sensitivity

claims.claim: The age-related deficit in time-compressed speech comprehension is attributed to central auditory temporal processing deficits combined with cognitive declines in processing speed, working memory, and executive function — disentangling multiple non-peripheral mechanisms.
claims.quote: age-related deficits in auditory temporal processing, coupled with senescent declines in cognitive abilities of speed of information processing, working memory, and executive function

claims.claim: Older adults retain rapid-adaptation/perceptual-learning capacity for time-compressed speech: when matched to younger listeners on baseline performance, they adapt at a comparable rate and magnitude over as few as 20 sentences, implying the deficit lies in baseline ability, not in the capacity to learn.
claims.quote: Both listener groups adapted to the trained stimuli rapidly and showed comparable rate and magnitude of learning over the course of 20 sentences.

claims.claim: Active training of older adults on time-compressed speech yields measurable but modest gains — on the order of ~6% change in time-compression ratio for speech-recognition training and ~25-30% in a passage-comprehension task.
claims.quote: approximately a 6% change in TCR for speech recognition training

claims.claim: Training benefits in older adults generalize poorly: unlike younger listeners, older adults fail to transfer learning to untrained sentences, limiting real-world trainability of speed-listening in old age.
claims.quote: older adults failed to show transfer to the untrained sentences, unlike the younger listeners

claims.claim: Speech-in-noise recognition (SRTn) follows an inverted-U lifespan trajectory peaking in early adulthood: it improves fastest from ages 7-20, reaches optimal performance during 21-40 years, then declines, with the curve's turning point estimated at 37.8 years (n=357, ages 7-90).
claims.quote: optimal speech perception performance occurred during early adulthood (21–40 years), with performance increasing at the fastest rate from 7 to 20 years old ... The turning point of this curve was estimated at 37.8 years, marking the age at which speech perception in noise begins to decline.

claims.claim: Cognition (processing speed and auditory working memory) dominates the age effect on speech-in-noise recognition over peripheral hearing sensitivity; early-onset hearing-threshold change does not meaningfully impair performance until cognitive decline begins, so the age penalty is driven by central/cognitive rather than peripheral factors.
claims.quote: This measurable early change in hearing sensitivity did not significantly impact speech perception in noise until the onset of an evident decline in cognitive abilities, emphasizing the importance of the latter to speech perception in noise

claims.claim: Hearing sensitivity (PTA4) begins to deteriorate in adolescence (turning point ~17 years), more than a decade before speech-in-noise performance peaks (~38 years), demonstrating that measurable audibility decline does not by itself drive the age-related comprehension decline.
claims.quote: The fitted curve demonstrated a gradual increase in hearing thresholds across the lifespan, with a turning point estimated at approximately 17 years of age.

claims.claim: A structural-equation model with age, cognition, and hearing as predictors (mediation framework) explained 61% of the individual variance in speech-reception thresholds in noise.
claims.quote: Altogether, the predictors in the model explained 61% of the individual variation in SRTn.

claims.claim: The peak-performance age for speech-in-noise (~37.8 years) coincides with the inflection points of auditory working memory (~38 years) and processing speed (~32-37 years), but precedes the clinical-hearing-loss threshold crossing (~68.7 years) by roughly 30 years, indicating cognitive rather than peripheral timing of the age effect.
claims.quote: The turning point of this curve was estimated at 37.8 years, marking the age at which speech perception in noise begins to decline.

claims.claim: The paper 'Effects of working memory capacity on understanding rate-altered speech' was published in Aging, Neuropsychology, and Cognition, Vol. 4, No. 2 (1997), p. 126, establishing it as a peer-reviewed primary research study within the Wingfield research program on aging and time-compressed speech.
claims.quote: Effects of working memory capacity on understanding rate-altered speech: Aging, Neuropsychology, and Cognition: Vol 4, No 2

claims.claim: This 1997 study sits within a research line showing that older listeners are disadvantaged relative to younger adults at fast/time-compressed speech rates, especially when linguistic and semantic cues are reduced or syntax is complex, consistent with age-related declines in processing speed and working memory.
claims.quote: Time-compressed speech has been used in the laboratory to investigate the effects of a decline in speed of processing and working memory abilities with age. Results generally show that older listeners are at a disadvantage compared to younger adult listeners for fast speech rates, particularly when the speech materials contain reduced linguistic and semantic cues or are syntactically complex.

result.evidence: CLAIM CONFIRMED, NOT REFUTED. The primary source (Gordon-Salant et al., Audiology Research 2011;1(1):e4, peer-reviewed, PMC4627162) supports every element of the claim verbatim. (1) Compression ratios: the paper states speech was "time-compressed by 30, 40, 50, and 60%" — exact match. (2) Main effects: "Significant main effects of age, hearing loss, and time compression ratio were observed, and there were no significant interactions" — exact match to the supporting quote. (3) Direction of age effect: explicitly stated "Older listeners performed more poorly than the younger listeners in all time compression conditions, but not in the uncompressed condition," and "older listeners experience considerable difficulty understanding speech presented at faster-than-normal rates, independent of the effects of hearing loss." This directly substantiates "older listeners performing worse than younger adults." Study design: 4 groups (younger/older x normal-hearing/matched mild-to-moderate sensorineural loss), so hearing loss IS controlled — and the age deficit persisted independently of hearing loss (additive, no interaction). No overreach: the claim is descriptive and conservative relative to the source. No contradicting evidence — this is a robustly replicated finding in auditory-aging literature (Gordon-Salant, Fitzgibbons, Wingfield). Source quality (peer-reviewed primary empirical study) matches the claim's modest strength. Not outdated (2011; a stable, established finding), not marketing. Minor note: the age deficit holds specifically in compressed conditions, not the uncompressed baseline — but the claim is explicitly about time-compressed speech, so it remains accurate.

Claim is faithfully supported by the source (PMC4627162, Gordon-Salant, Fitzgibbons & Yeni-Komshian, "Auditory Temporal Processing and Aging," Audiology Research, 2011). WebFetch of the article confirms verbatim: speech "time-compressed by 30, 40, 50, and 60%" and "Significant main effects of age, hearing loss, and time compression ratio were observed, and there were no significant interactions."
