Older listeners performed worse than younger ("older listeners experience considerable difficulty understanding speech presented at faster-than-normal rates, independent of the effects of hearing loss"), and the design explicitly compared younger-NH, older-NH, and older-HI groups, so hearing loss was measured/controlled — the age effect persists independent of audibility (effects additive, not interactive). No overreach: the claim's three main effects and direction match the quote exactly. Source quality matches claim strength: peer-reviewed paper by a leading auditory-aging research group. Not refuted on overreach, contradiction, source quality, datedness, or marketing grounds. Independent corroboration: PMC9557069, PMC6364265, and PubMed 8972446 all report the same direction (older adults worse at higher compression, central/non-peripheral age effects). One minor caveat: some other studies (e.g., the WebSearch summary) report an age-by-compression INTERACTION (older adults disproportionately worse at 50-60%), whereas this specific experiment found additive main effects with no interaction — but the claim only asserts main effects, so this does not refute it.
result.counterSource: Some studies report an age x compression interaction (older adults disproportionately impaired at 50-60%, near-ceiling parity at 40%) rather than the purely additive main effects in this paper (per WebSearch summary citing PMC9557069 / PubMed 8972446); however this nuances rather than contradicts the claim, which only asserts main effects.

result.evidence: VERIFIED, NOT REFUTED. The claim is a faithful, near-verbatim representation of the source (PMC4627162, "Auditory Temporal Processing and Aging: Implications for Speech Understanding of Older People," Gordon-Salant/Fitzgibbons/Yeni-Komshian). WebFetch of the article confirmed both load-bearing facts: (a) stimuli were presented "uncompressed (original) rate, and time-compressed by 30, 40, 50, and 60%," and (b) "significant main effects of age, hearing loss, and time compression ratio were observed, and there were no significant interactions." The article also states "older listeners experience considerable difficulty understanding speech presented at faster-than-normal rates, independent of the effects of hearing loss," directly backing "older listeners performing worse than younger adults." Note the "no significant interactions" detail STRENGTHENS rather than weakens the claim: the age deficit held consistently across all four compression ratios. Source quality is sufficient — peer-reviewed primary article by leading researchers in auditory aging. Independent WebSearch found NO contradicting evidence; instead multiple corroborating peer-reviewed studies (ASHA pubs.asha.org/10.1044/1092-4388(2001/056); PubMed 8972446; Frontiers 2022 10.3389/fnagi.2022.887581; PMC10233765 on age-related hearing loss accelerating fast-speech decline) all confirm independent main effects of age and hearing loss on time-compressed speech recognition. The finding is foundational and replicated, so age of publication does not render it outdated. No marketing/press-release/forum issues. The only minor inaccuracy is metadata-level (the WebFetch returned slightly inconsistent journal naming, but the title and authors are confirmed) and does not affect the substantive claim.

CLAIM SUPPORTED. Primary source PMC4627162 states verbatim that "Older blind participants performed significantly better than the older sighted participants in all time compression conditions in quiet" and "There were no performance differences between the older blind participants and the younger sighted participants in any condition," and concludes the supporting quote nearly word-for-word: the difficulty older listeners have with rapid speech "is not inherent to the aging process, but rather, can be ameliorated through training and experience with speech presented at faster-than-normal rates." The claim accurately restates this, not an overreach. The empirical basis is Gordon-Salant & Friedman (2010, J Speech Lang Hear Res, PMID 20689022, ~10/group), which selected for NORMAL HEARING in all groups (peripheral audibility controlled) and reported r=.82-.88 between hours of fast-speech (audiobook) listening and time-compressed speech performance. The trainability premise is independently corroborated: older adults aged 65-91 show substantial perceptual-learning gains training on time-compressed speech (PMC7880302; PLOS One 2017 e0176488), and the blind ultra-fast-speech literature (Dietrich/Hertrich, Frontiers) shows both early- AND late-blind acquire ~20 syl/s comprehension, indicating experience-driven acquisition rather than congenital-only effect. Source quality matches claim strength (primary peer-reviewed). SKEPTICAL CAVEATS that fall short of refutation: (a) the blind comparison is cross-sectional/correlational, not a randomized training trial, so blindness-related cortical plasticity cannot be fully separated from training experience; (b) the older-blind advantage is largest in quiet and attenuated in noise; (c) training gains in older adults show limited generalization to untrained stimuli. But the claim is hedged ("indicating... trainable") and aligns with the authors' own conclusion plus convergent independent evidence; no credible source disputes it.
result.counterSource: No refuting source found. Closest qualifiers: PMC7880302 and Frontiers (fpsyg.2015.02066) note older-adult training gains have limited generalization to untrained sounds, and noise conditions reduce the blind advantage — these qualify but do not contradict the trainability claim.

result.evidence: SUPPORTED by primary source. The claim accurately reports Gordon-Salant & Friedman (2011), "Recognition of Rapid Speech by Blind and Sighted Older Adults," J Speech Lang Hear Res 54:622-631 (n=10/group, all normal hearing: older blind, older sighted, younger sighted). Both quantitative legs verify: (a) "no performance differences between the older blind participants and the younger sighted participants in any condition" (= as well as younger sighted), and (b) older blind "performed significantly better than the older sighted participants in all time compression conditions in quiet and in the uncompressed and 40% time-compressed condition in noise" (40/50/60% TCR tested). Peripheral hearing loss is CONTROLLED — all groups normal-hearing — so the advantage is not explained by presbycusis, strengthening the central/cognitive trainability interpretation. The supporting quote ("not inherent to the aging process... can be ameliorated through training and experience") is the authors' own stated conclusion, so the claim faithfully reports the source rather than overreaching. CAVEAT (not sufficient to refute, 1 of needed 2): the design is CROSS-SECTIONAL/CORRELATIONAL, not a randomized training intervention — blind participants self-selected into heavy fast audiobook use (correlation r=.82-.88 between listening hours and performance), so strict causality ("trainable rather than inherent") is an inference, and self-selection/other compensatory advantages of blindness aren't fully excluded. However independent training-intervention studies corroborate that older adults DO improve on time-compressed speech (e.g., PMC11075136 cochlear-implant training at advanced ages; PMC7880302 review showing intact perceptual learning in ages 65-91, albeit with limited generalization to untrained stimuli). Source quality (peer-reviewed JSLHR primary study + review) matches claim strength; not outdated (2011 finding consistent with 2020s literature); not marketing. Only one legitimate qualifier found, below the 2-refutation kill threshold.
result.counterSource: Cross-sectional design caveat (self-selection, not RCT) — and PMC7880302 / S0378595520303257 note older-adult training gains show LIMITED transfer/generalization to untrained stimuli, partially qualifying the strength of "trainable." Central auditory aging is still real (older > younger penalty on fast speech), but it is ameliorable, not eliminated, by experience.

result.evidence: The claim is well-supported and faithfully quoted. PRIMARY SOURCE (Gordon-Salant, Fitzgibbons & Yeni-Komshian, "Auditory Temporal Processing and Aging," 2011, PMC4627162) — a peer-reviewed literature review from a leading auditory-aging research group — directly states "older listeners experience considerable difficulty understanding speech presented at faster-than-normal rates, independent of the effects of hearing loss" and "the effects of hearing loss and age operate independently, and therefore may become additive." The quote matches the claim, no overreach. CORROBORATION (independent, peer-reviewed): (1) The age penalty on time-compressed speech PERSISTS after controlling for audibility — confirmed in PMC4003736 (older listeners with normal-range hearing still struggle) and PMC8972446 (Gordon-Salant & Fitzgibbons 1997: "effect of age group persisted even after controlling the effect of hearing threshold"). (2) Springer JARO 2018 (s10162-018-00703-1) attributes the age effect to central neural processing + perceptuomotor speed + executive function, not audibility. SOURCE QUALITY: appropriate (peer-reviewed reviews + empirical studies, not marketing/forum/press release). NOT OUTDATED: the core finding (central/cognitive age deficit beyond peripheral hearing) is replicated through 2018-2023. ONE NUANCE (qualifies but does not refute): the strict "additive" mechanism is debated — PMC10233765 (2022) is titled "hearing loss ACCELERATES the decline" and a Frontiers review (PMC8891456) calls it "a complex interplay... rather than simply an additive combination," favoring interaction over pure additivity. But this debate concerns only the additive-vs-interactive mechanism; the load-bearing claim — that the age penalty is INDEPENDENT of and PERSISTS BEYOND peripheral hearing loss — is robustly confirmed by every source. The "additive" wording is the primary source's own hypothesis, accurately quoted.
result.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8891456/ (Frontiers review) and PMC10233765 (2022) frame the relationship as a complex interplay/acceleration rather than strictly additive — a partial qualification of the "additive" wording, but not of the core "independent of / persists after controlling for hearing loss" claim, which they also support.

result.evidence: Claim is well-supported and corroborated. PRIMARY SOURCE verified: PMC4627162 is Gordon-Salant, Fitzgibbons & Yeni-Komshian (2011), "Auditory Temporal Processing and Aging: Implications for Speech Understanding of Older People," a peer-reviewed review by the leading research group in this field. The supporting quote is reproduced ACCURATELY: the article states older listeners have "considerable difficulty understanding speech presented at faster-than-normal rates, independent of the effects of hearing loss" and that "older listeners with hearing loss are at a considerable disadvantage... because the effects of hearing loss and age operate independently, and therefore may become additive" (with "Significant main effects of age, hearing loss, and time compression ratio were observed, and there were no significant interactions"). The claim faithfully paraphrases this — no overreach. INDEPENDENT CORROBORATION from multiple peer-reviewed sources: (a) Gordon-Salant & Fitzgibbons (2001, JSLHR) found hearing-loss effects "independent of the aging effects"; (b) cochlear-implant studies (PMC9557069, Frontiers 2022) that bypass peripheral hearing still show age effects on time-compressed speech, isolating central/age factors; (c) PMC10233765 and others confirm "people with age-related hearing loss suffer from speech understanding difficulties even after correcting for differences in hearing audibility," tied to central temporal processing. The finding has been replicated 1996–2023, so it is NOT outdated for this slow-moving field, and is NOT a marketing/press claim. MINOR QUALIFICATION (does not refute): the mechanism is debated — some studies report an age x compression-ratio INTERACTION (age penalty grows at higher compression), which slightly qualifies strict "additivity," and there is debate over whether central-auditory vs cognitive (processing-speed/working-memory) factors dominate (one finding: cognitive processing speed did NOT predict time-compression effects). But these concern the mechanism, not the core claim about the age effect persisting independent of audibility, which is robust. Two of three refutation tests (contradiction, low-quality source, outdated) fail; only a partial mechanistic nuance applies. Claim stands.
result.counterSource: No credible source contradicts the core claim. The only partial qualification: some studies (e.g., reviews citing age x time-compression-ratio interactions, and PMC6377055 noting time-compressed speech does not fully predict natural fast-rate speech) nuance strict additivity and mechanism, but none dispute that the age effect persists after controlling for hearing loss.

result.evidence: All load-bearing facts verified verbatim against the primary peer-reviewed source (Gordon-Salant et al., "Recognition of time-compressed speech does not predict recognition of natural fast-rate speech by older listeners," J. Acoust. Soc. Am., PMC6377055). Confirmed: younger group 19-22 (mean 20.4), older group 66-76 (mean 68.8); both screened to pure-tone thresholds <=25 dB HL ("normal hearing sensitivity"). In the noise condition (+10 dB SNR), the study reports "the YNH listeners outperformed the ONH listeners for 40TC (p=0.012)" [time-compressed], "and fast speech (p=0.005)" [naturally fast], "but there was no difference between groups for the normal rate (p=0.52)." These three p-values match the claim exactly. The article's stated conclusion supports the supporting quote: older listeners are more detrimentally affected by fast speech, especially naturally fast speech. Source quality (primary peer-reviewed JASA article) matches claim strength; not marketing/benchmark/forum. One nuance, not a refutation: groups were not identical in audibility — older listeners had poorer 4000 Hz thresholds (RE 13.6, LE 17.27 dB HL) vs younger (RE 6.9, LE 5.38 dB HL), though all remained within normal range; authors attributed the deficit to central/cognitive factors (processing speed, attention, working memory) and did NOT substantively control for or rule out the residual 4 kHz audibility difference. The claim's wording ("persists despite both groups having normal hearing, <=25 dB HL") is accurate as stated and does not assert identical thresholds, so this caveat qualifies rather than refutes. No contradicting evidence found.

CLAIM SURVIVES SKEPTICAL REVIEW. (1) Quote support: verbatim match in the source (Gordon-Salant, Fitzgibbons & Yeni-Komshian 2011, "Auditory Temporal Processing and Aging," a peer-reviewed review by leading researchers in the field): "older listeners experience considerable difficulty understanding speech presented at faster-than-normal rates, independent of the effects of hearing loss" and "the effects of hearing loss and age operate independently, and therefore may become additive." The underlying data (Gordon-Salant & Fitzgibbons 1993) showed "Significant main effects of age, hearing loss, and time compression ratio... and there were no significant interactions" — directly supporting separate/additive (non-interactive) effects of age and hearing loss. (2) Independent corroboration that the age penalty persists after controlling for audibility: Frontiers/PMC9557069 (2022) tested cochlear-implant users (bypassing the cochlea) and found older CI users still performed worse — "deficits... may be primarily affected by age-related declines in central auditory processing rather than solely related to peripheral age-related hearing loss." PMC6364265 (Gordon-Salant et al.) found adding audiometric thresholds "failed to significantly improve model fit (χ²(1)=0.130, p=0.718)" and that age-group variance "is not explained by individual differences in audiometric thresholds." (3) Source quality matches claim strength: authoritative domain experts, peer-reviewed, and replicated by later studies. (4) Not outdated: 2019/2022 studies reinforce rather than overturn the 2011 review. (5) Not marketing/forum/benchmark. ONE FLAG: the claim states "additive" more confidently than the source's hedged "may become additive," and a separate literature (rate × linguistic complexity, e.g., grammatical-complexity studies) finds MULTIPLICATIVE/interactive effects — but that concerns rate×complexity, not age×hearing-loss, so it does not contradict the specific claim. Also, mechanistically the persisting age effect is attributed to central/cognitive factors (perceptuomotor speed mediates and renders age nonsignificant; auditory neural processing/P1 latency; executive function), so "independent of peripheral hearing loss" is correct but the age penalty is NOT mechanism-free — it is driven by central/cognitive aging, which the claim does not misstate.
result.counterSource: Partial nuance (not a refutation): rate-by-linguistic-complexity effects are reported as multiplicative/interactive rather than additive (PMC8891456, "Speech Perception in Older Adults"; Wingfield-line work), but this concerns speech rate × grammatical complexity, not the age × hearing-loss additivity the claim asserts. Mechanistic qualifier: PMC6364265 shows perceptuomotor processing speed mediates the age effect (age becomes nonsignificant once it is added), so the persisting age penalty is central/cognitive, not peripheral — consistent with, not contrary to, the claim.

result.evidence: VERIFIED against the underlying primary study (Gordon-Salant & Friedman 2010, "Recognition of rapid speech by blind and sighted older adults," J Speech Lang Hear Res; PMC3034112), which is the basis for the review quote in PMC4627162. EMPIRICAL PART confirmed precisely: groups of n=10 each (younger sighted 18-30y; older sighted 60-80y mean 66.7y; older blind 60-80y mean 67.0y), all with pure-tone thresholds <=25 dB HL 500-4000 Hz (audibility CONTROLLED and matched across older groups). "Older blind listeners recognized all time-compressed speech stimuli significantly better than older sighted listeners in quiet"; "no significant performance differences between young sighted listeners and older blind listeners in any condition." INTERPRETIVE PART ("trainable not inherent") is the authors' own conclusion, matching the supporting quote: "Age-related difficulty in understanding time-compressed speech is not an inevitable consequence of aging. Rather, frequent listening...may be a useful technique to minimize age-related slowing." Experience effect is large: r=.82 (quiet) and .88 (noise) between weekly fast-speech listening hours and performance at 60% compression (>70% of variance). CORROBORATION: independent controlled training studies confirm older adults retain perceptual-learning plasticity for time-compressed speech (PMC7880302 review; PMC4737899; PMC11075136 shows even aged cochlear-implant users benefit). No contradicting source found. CAVEAT (does not refute): the blind/sighted study is CROSS-SECTIONAL/CORRELATIONAL, not a training RCT, so "trainable" is an inference subject to possible self-selection; but the claim's hedge word "indicating" is appropriate and the inference is independently supported by controlled-training literature. Source quality (peer-reviewed primary + review) matches the claim strength; not outdated for this slow-moving field; not marketing.

Claim is well-supported by the primary source (Gordon-Salant, Zion & Espy-Wilson, JASA 2014, "Recognition of time-compressed speech does not predict recognition of natural fast-rate speech by older listeners," PMC6377055). Every quantitative element verified against the article text: (a) age ranges exact — younger 19-22 (mean 20.4), older 66-76 (mean 68.8); (b) hearing status exact — both groups <=25 dB HL at 250-4000 Hz, characterized by authors as "clearly within the range of normal hearing"; (c) p-values exact and in noise — time-compressed p=0.012, natural-fast p=0.005, normal-rate p=0.52 (no group difference). Abstract conclusion confirms: "older listeners are at a much greater disadvantage when listening to natural fast speech." The supporting quote ("the older listeners were more detrimentally affected by the fast speech rate, especially naturally fast speech, compared to the younger listeners") accurately reflects the paper. This is a peer-reviewed primary study by a leading researcher in the field, and the finding is replicated across the broader literature (Frontiers 2022 PMC9557069; Journal of Hearing Science age-on-time-compressed study; related Gordon-Salant 2007 PMID 17905904). One nuance, NOT sufficient to refute: the paper did NOT statistically control for a significant between-group difference at 4000 Hz (older 13.6/17.3 dB HL vs younger 6.9/5.4 dB HL, p<0.01), so the single study does not fully rule out a residual audibility confound. However, the claim's wording ("persists despite both groups having normal hearing sensitivity") matches the paper's own framing and does NOT falsely assert audibility was statistically controlled, so it is not an overreach. Claim is current (2014, stable established finding in a non-fast-moving field) and not a marketing/press claim.

Every specific figure in the claim is confirmed verbatim against the primary source (PMC6377055, Gordon-Salant et al., Frontiers/peer-reviewed). Younger group 19-22 yrs (mean 20.4, n=13), older 66-76 yrs (mean 68.8, n=12) — matches exactly. Both groups met "pure-tone air conduction thresholds <=25 dB HL" — matches the claim's normal-hearing criterion verbatim. P-values match the source word-for-word: in noise, "YNH listeners outperformed the ONH listeners for 40TC (p=0.012) and fast speech (p=0.005), but there was no difference between groups for the normal rate (p=0.52)." Supporting quote ("older listeners were more detrimentally affected by the fast speech rate, especially naturally fast speech") is accurate and matches the conclusion. WebSearch for contradicting evidence found NONE — instead multiple independent peer-reviewed sources (PMC9557069 cochlear-implant study, PMC4906303, PMC4627162, PMC10233765) corroborate that the age penalty on time-compressed/fast speech persists after controlling for hearing sensitivity and reflects central auditory/cognitive processing, not just peripheral presbycusis. Source quality is sufficient: peer-reviewed primary study directly matching the claim's strength. Minor caveat (not refuting): the study did NOT formally statistically equate audibility — older listeners had higher 4 kHz thresholds (13.6/17.27 vs 6.9/5.38 dB HL, p<0.01) though still within the <=25 dB HL normal range — so "persists despite normal hearing" means "both met clinical normal-hearing criteria," not "audibility was covaried out." The claim's wording correctly scopes this to <=25 dB HL, so it is not an overreach.

The claim is accurately supported by a peer-reviewed primary source. The cited review article (Gordon-Salant et al., PMC4627162) states verbatim: "The r values were .82 and .88 for time-compressed speech in quiet and in noise, respectively." These trace to the original primary study, Gordon-Salant, Yeni-Komshian & Fitzgibbons (2010), "Recognition of Rapid Speech by Blind and Sighted Older Adults," J Speech Lang Hear Res (PMC3034112), which reports: "significant and strong correlations between the number of hours/week listening to rapid speech and speech recognition scores for the most difficult TCR conditions (60%) in quiet (r = .82, p<.01) and noise (r = .88, p<.01)." The correlated variables (hours of fast-rate listening vs. 60% time-compressed speech recognition), the group (n=10 older normal-hearing blind listeners), the direction, and both magnitudes match the claim exactly. Both correlations were statistically significant at p<.01. The ONLY weakness: the claim's framing as "dose-response of speed-listening training" overstates the design — this is a cross-sectional self-report correlation (small N=10, only 8 reporting experience), not an experimental training manipulation, and the original authors hedge it as "intriguing findings" that "might improve" processing. But this is an interpretive characterization, not a factual error; the quantitative core (r=.82/.88, p<.01, blind listeners) is correct, current, and primary-sourced. Source quality matches claim strength.
result.counterSource: No contradicting source found. Caveat from the same primary source (PMC3034112): correlation is cross-sectional, n=10 (8 with relevant experience), framed by authors as exploratory ("intriguing," "might improve"), so the claim's "dose-response of training" wording slightly overstates an experimental design that was not run.

result.evidence: VERIFIED against both the citing review (PMC4627162, Gordon-Salant et al.) and the underlying PRIMARY study (PMC3034112: Gordon-Salant & Friedman, 2010, "Recognition of Rapid Speech by Blind and Sighted Older Adults," J Speech Lang Hear Res — peer-reviewed JSLHR). The exact correlations are real and accurately stated: r=.82 (60% time-compressed speech in quiet) and r=.88 (in noise), both p<.01, between blind listeners' recognition performance at the most degraded compression and the number of hours they reported listening to faster-than-normal-rate materials (e.g., books on tape). The supporting quote ("The r values were .82 and .88 for time-compressed speech in quiet and in noise, respectively") matches verbatim. Variables, direction, and conditions in the claim are all correct. Source quality (peer-reviewed primary study) matches the claim strength; the finding is a fixed historical data point, not outdated; no marketing/forum origin; no credible source contradicts it. CAVEAT (does not rise to refutation): the analysis was exploratory/post-hoc on a SMALL sample (n=10 blind participants); the authors themselves hedge ("Additional study with a larger number of participants...may clarify this observation" and "Whether or not this skill would generalize...is unclear"). The claim's editorial phrase "quantifying the dose-response of speed-listening training" slightly over-attributes CAUSALITY to what is a cross-sectional correlation — but the claim's main verb is correctly "correlates strongly," so the core quantitative assertion is well-supported. The claim should ideally note n=10 and the exploratory nature, but the numbers and what they measure are exactly right.
result.counterSource: No contradicting source found. Underlying primary study (PMC3034112, Gordon-Salant & Friedman 2010, JSLHR) confirms r=.82/.88, p<.01, but flags n=10 and exploratory/post-hoc design with author caveats on generalizability/causality.

result.evidence: VERIFIED against the primary empirical source. The r values originate from Gordon-Salant, Yeni-Komshian & Fitzgibbons, "Recognition of rapid speech by blind and sighted older adults" (J Speech Lang Hear Res, 2010; PMC3034112), cited in the review at PMC4627162. The paper states verbatim: "The results of these analyses revealed significant and strong correlations between the number of hours/week listening to rapid speech and speech recognition scores for the most difficult TCR conditions (60%) in quiet (r = .82, p<.01) and noise (r = .88, p<.01)." This is an EXACT match to the claim's figures (r=.82 quiet, .88 noise) and the correlated variable (hours listening to faster-than-normal-rate speech materials, "e.g., books on tape"). The measure was self-reported average hours/week over the past year among older blind listeners (n=10, of whom 8 had experience, range 4-40 hrs/week). The p<.01 significance (omitted in the claim's quote but present in the source) strengthens rather than weakens it; r=.88 implies ~77% of variance explained. Two minor imprecisions do NOT rise to refutation: (1) the metric is hours-PER-WEEK, not cumulative "number of hours" as the claim loosely implies; (2) "dose-response of speed-listening training" is an interpretive overreach — the data are correlational/observational (habitual self-reported listening), not a controlled training intervention, so they associate exposure with skill but do not establish trained causation. The core quantitative claim is accurate, peer-reviewed, primary-sourced, and not outdated. Caveat for downstream use: small sample (n=10) and correlational design mean it should be framed as "exposure strongly associated with" rather than proven training dose-response.
result.counterSource: No contradicting source found. Note the claim's cited source (PMC4627162) is a 2011 review; the underlying primary data are from Gordon-Salant et al. 2010 (PMC3034112), which reports identical figures with p<.01.

result.evidence: The supporting quote is accurate and the study (PMC6377055, Gordon-Salant et al., "Recognition of time-compressed speech does not predict recognition of natural fast-rate speech by older listeners") did test 40% time-compressed and natural fast speech in young (19-22) vs older (66-76) normal-hearing adults, attributing deficits to "cognitive processing resources (i.e., processing speed, attention, working memory)." However, the claim OVERREACHES on its load-bearing phrase "demonstrating the age penalty after controlling for audibility." The study did NOT control for audibility statistically — it only applied an inclusion criterion (<=25 dB HL at 250-4000 Hz). Critically, older listeners had SIGNIFICANTLY worse 4000 Hz thresholds even within the "normal" range (older RE 13.6 / LE 17.3 dB HL vs younger RE 6.9 / LE 5.4 dB HL, p<0.01), and the paper performed no covariate adjustment for this and offered no caution. "Controlling for audibility" implies statistical control that did not occur; matched inclusion criteria are not the same. Furthermore, the <=25 dB HL criterion extended only to 4000 Hz with no extended-high-frequency (>8 kHz) testing. The broader literature undercuts the implied conclusion: the WHO lowered the normal/mild boundary to 20 dB HL (PMC7212814), and extended-high-frequency thresholds in clinically "normal" ears (<=25 dB HL conventionally) still predict speech-recognition difficulty including time-compressed words (PMC9010339, PMC11493509). Thus the peripheral contribution is NOT excluded, and the claim that the age penalty was demonstrated "after controlling for audibility" is a methodological overstatement/misread of a matched-inclusion design.
result.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6377055/ (older listeners' 4000 Hz thresholds significantly worse, p<0.01, with no statistical control for audibility); https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7212814/ (WHO lowered normal boundary to 20 dB HL); https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9010339/ and https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11493509/ (extended-high-frequency and within-normal thresholds still predict speech difficulty, so <=25 dB HL criterion does not fully control audibility)

result.evidence: REFUTED as an overreach/misread of PMC6377055 ("Recognition of time-compressed speech does not predict recognition of natural fast-rate speech by older listeners"; YNH aged 19-22 vs ONH aged 66-76). The supporting quote ("...normal hearing sensitivity...pure-tone air conduction thresholds <=25 dB HL...250 to 4000 Hz") is accurate verbatim, but the inferences the claim draws are NOT supported by the study: (1) NO cognitive factors were measured or statistically controlled — processing speed, attention, and working memory were only mentioned conceptually in the Introduction citing prior literature; the study ran no cognitive tests, so it cannot have "attributed" the deficit to them. (2) The paper makes NO conclusion that the deficit is central "rather than peripheral audibility" and never claims to "demonstrate the age penalty after controlling for audibility" — that phrasing is the claim-writer's construction. (3) Audibility was NOT actually controlled: the two age groups were NOT threshold-matched; older listeners had significantly WORSE 4000 Hz thresholds (~13.6-17.3 vs ~5.4-6.9 dB HL, p<0.01). A <=25 dB HL ceiling bounds but does not equate audibility. (4) Independent literature (Frontiers/PMC4292733; hearingreview "Beyond Audibility") establishes that supra-threshold auditory processing differences persist with "normal" (<=25 dB HL) audiograms, so the <=25 dB HL criterion does NOT constitute "controlling for audibility." The paper's actual main finding concerned the dissociation between time-compressed vs. natural fast speech, a different question. The claim conflates "all participants had clinically-normal audiograms" with "audibility was controlled and central/cognitive causation was demonstrated" — neither is true in this source.
result.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6377055/ (the cited source itself: no cognitive measures taken; groups not threshold-matched, older worse at 4000 Hz p<0.01; no "controlling for audibility" conclusion) ; https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4292733/ and https://hearingreview.com/resource-center/expert-insight/beyond-audibility-role-supra-threshold-auditory-processing-cognition-presbycusis (supra-threshold/audibility differences persist despite normal audiograms, so <=25 dB HL does not equal controlling audibility)

result.evidence: Claim is accurately supported by the primary source: Jenstad LM & Souza PE (2007), "Temporal envelope changes of compression and speech rate: combined effects on recognition for older adults," J Speech Lang Hear Res, DOI 10.1044/1092-4388(2007/078), PMID 17905900. The PubMed abstract confirms every quantitative detail: young-old group 62-74 yrs (n=11), old-old group 75-88 yrs (n=14), both with mild-to-moderate hearing loss (groups did not differ in hearing loss, cognition, working memory, or health), low-predictability sentences tested including a 50% time-compressed condition. The abstract states verbatim: "There was no difference in performance between age groups, or any interactions involving age" and "For this sample tested, the effect was not age related within the age range tested here." The interpretive gloss "no further decline across this older age band" faithfully restates the authors' own conclusion (not an overreach). Adversarial search for contradicting evidence instead corroborated the pattern: the broader literature shows age effects on time-compressed speech are a younger-vs-older/threshold effect emerging by ~50s (PMC6364265; PMC9557069), with within-older-band comparisons (middle-aged vs older; "no significant differences in performance between the MCI and OCI groups") showing similar performance — consistent with, not contradicting, the claim. Source is primary, peer-reviewed, and matches the claim's modest, well-hedged strength. Only minor caveat: it is a null result from a small sample (n=11/14) with limited power, but the claim does not overstate beyond the authors' own wording. Age (2007) is not disqualifying — this is foundational psychoacoustic/aging work in a slow-moving subfield, still routinely cited.
result.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9557069/ — corroborates rather than refutes: within-older-band comparisons show similar performance ("no significant differences between MCI and OCI groups"); age effect is a threshold emerging by ~50s, not a continuous decline across older bands.

result.evidence: REFUTED on three independent grounds. Source: Gordon-Salant, Zion & Espy-Wilson (2014), J. Acoust. Soc. Am. 136:EL268-EL274 (PMC6377055). (1) AUDIBILITY WAS NOT CONTROLLED — the claim's core premise ("after controlling for audibility") is false. Older adults had significantly WORSE thresholds at 4000 Hz (older RE 13.6/LE 17.27 dB HL vs younger RE 6.9/LE 5.38 dB HL, p<0.01). The paper applied NO statistical control for audibility; both groups merely fell under the nominal <=25 dB HL ceiling, which does not equate audibility. (2) THE CENTRAL/COGNITIVE ATTRIBUTION IS SPECULATIVE, NOT DEMONSTRATED — the actual text is hedged: deficits are 'exacerbated in noise, PERHAPS BECAUSE the cognitive processing resources (i.e., processing speed, attention, working memory)... are more limited among older than younger listeners.' This is an interpretive mechanism citing prior literature, not an original finding, and the study did not dissociate central from peripheral factors. The claim's phrase 'demonstrating the age penalty after controlling for audibility' overstates a 'perhaps' into a demonstration. (3) WRONG SPEECH TYPE / CONTRADICTS PAPER'S THESIS — the paper's title and conclusion is that recognition of TIME-COMPRESSED speech does NOT predict natural fast-rate speech. For time-compressed speech in quiet there was NO significant overall age main effect (only a context interaction for anomalous low-context sentences). The robust age deficit was for NATURAL-FAST speech, not the time-compressed 'speed listening' at issue. The claim conflates 'fast-speech' generally with the controlled audibility/central-factor narrative the paper does not support.
result.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6377055/ (the source itself: hearing thresholds differed by age p<0.01, no audibility control; cognitive mechanism stated only as 'perhaps because'; title states TC speech does NOT predict natural fast speech)

result.evidence: Claim is well-supported by the primary peer-reviewed source: Jenstad & Souza (2007), "Temporal Envelope Changes of Compression and Speech Rate: Combined Effects on Recognition for Older Adults," J Speech Lang Hear Res 50(5):1123-38 (PMID 17905900; the DOI in the claim resolves to this paper). Every specific in the claim is verified verbatim: young-old group 62-74 yrs (n=11) and old-old group 75-88 yrs (n=14); participants had mild-to-moderate hearing loss; conditions included 50% time-compressed low-predictability sentences; and the result was "There was no difference in performance between age groups, or any interactions involving age," with the authors concluding "For this sample tested, the effect was not age related within the age range tested here." The supporting quote matches the paper. The interpretive gloss "no further decline across this older age band" is the authors' own conclusion and is appropriately hedged in the claim to the tested range. No contradicting evidence found; the broader literature corroborates it — a Gordon-Salant study using age bands 55-64/65-74/75-84 with pure-tone thresholds matched within 5 dB found no significant time-compressed-speech recognition differences once hearing sensitivity was controlled. Minor weaknesses (small n, restricted older-only range) are inherent caveats the claim acknowledges, not refutations. Source quality (primary, peer-reviewed) matches the modest, descriptive strength of the claim; finding is foundational and not in a fast-moving field, so 2007 date is not disqualifying.

The claim is accurately supported by its primary source (PMC5786310, Dispaldro et al., "Don't speak too fast! Processing of fast rate speech in children with SLI"). Verified quote from the article: "The same analysis conducted in TD children with Age as a fixed factor also showed that this variable did not significantly affect d′ in the normal, natural fast and time-compressed conditions" (and similarly for the SLI group). Participant ages were 8-13 (mean 10.9), n=16 per group; speech rates were normal 6.76 syll/sec vs natural-fast/time-compressed 9.15 syll/sec (1.35x). So part 1 of the claim ("age within 8-13 did not significantly predict performance") is literally correct.

I attempted to refute via the most relevant contradicting study, PMC6238820 ("Ongoing maturation in the time-compressed speech test"), which found a SIGNIFICANT age effect (disyllables: age accounted for 57.7-64.4% of variance; p<0.001) with young adults outperforming children. However, this does NOT contradict the narrow claim: that study's age correlations were computed on all 60 subjects COMBINED (8-10yo children + 16-24yo adults), capturing a child-vs-adult gap, NOT a within-8-13 trajectory. WebFetch confirmed: "The study does not examine a fine-grained developmental trajectory within childhood... no separate correlation coefficients reported for the children-only subset." Thus the documented continued maturation occurs between childhood and adulthood, consistent with the claim's explicit caveat ("did not stratify a fine developmental trajectory").

The hedged inference "substantially functional by age 8" is also defensible: 8-10yo children performed well above chance (e.g., ~39.7% vs ~20% chance on disyllables) even though below adult levels. The claim correctly avoids asserting full maturity by age 8 and flags the trajectory limitation. Claim is current (~2018 developmental science, stable field), from a peer-reviewed primary source, accurately quoted, and appropriately scoped. Caveat for downstream use: peripheral hearing was screened (normal-hearing children) but the small n=16/group limits power to detect a subtle within-range age effect.
result.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6238820/ (found significant age effects, but only child-vs-adult, not within 8-13; therefore does not refute the narrow claim)

result.evidence: Source verified: PMC5786310 (Roy et al.) is a peer-reviewed primary study, N=32 children aged 8-13 (16 SLI, 16 age-matched TD), semantic plausibility judgment on naturally-fast (9.15 syl/s) and artificially time-compressed sentences. The supporting quote is verbatim and correctly applied: "The regression analysis including age as a fixed factor did not reveal any significant influence of this variable on performance in the TD and SLI groups." So the claim's factual core (age 8-13 did not significantly predict fast/compressed-speech comprehension) is directly supported. The interpretive add-on ("already substantially functional by age 8") is a soft overreach but stays defensible: the authors used age as a confound covariate (not a developmental variable), the null is underpowered (N=16/group), and they explicitly flagged this as a limitation, recommending future 8-10 vs 11-13 stratification ("it may be important for future studies...to systematically compare age ranges"). Crucially, the claim ITSELF includes that exact hedge ("though the study did not stratify a fine developmental trajectory"), matching the authors' caveat, so it does not misrepresent the paper. No contradicting evidence found that 8-year-olds are non-functional at fast speech. The claim is supported, current, and source quality matches strength — does not meet refutation bar despite the underpowered-null caveat. Note: task is semantic judgment ('does the sentence make sense'), a reasonable proxy for comprehension; peripheral hearing was screened (normal hearing), not a confound here.
result.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5786310/

result.evidence: The literal quote is verbatim-accurate in PMC5786310 ("The regression analysis including age as a fixed factor did not reveal any significant influence of this variable on performance in the TD and SLI groups"), but the claim's load-bearing INFERENCE ("the ability is already substantially functional by age 8") is an overreach the authors never made and is contradicted by stronger evidence. (1) UNDERPOWERED NULL: the TD group was only n=16 across a wide 8-13 range, with the DV being d-prime on a semantic-incongruity judgment task (not audiobook-style comprehension). The authors explicitly treat the developmental trajectory as OPEN, calling for "a larger number of participants to systematically compare age ranges (i.e., 8-10 vs. 11-13 years old) to investigate the developmental trajectory" — i.e., absence of a significant age effect in a tiny sample is not evidence the ability has matured. (2) DIRECT CONTRADICTION: A more apt, better-powered study, "Ongoing maturation in the time-compressed speech test" (PMC6238820), finds age is a STRONG predictor of time-compressed speech performance, explaining ~64.4% of right-ear and ~57.7% of left-ear variance (disyllable modality); young adults (16-24) significantly outperform children (8-10), and the ability "is still maturing through childhood" and is NOT adult-like by age 8-10. (3) CORROBORATING: speech-in-noise/auditory-closure abilities reach adult-like scores only ~10-13 years, not 8. Thus the inference that fast-speech comprehension is "substantially functional by age 8" is refuted by the maturation literature, even though the quoted null result itself is real.
result.counterSource: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6238820/ (Ongoing maturation in the time-compressed speech test — age a strong predictor, ~57-64% of variance; children 8-10 not adult-like, ability still maturing into adolescence/young adulthood)

result.evidence: Every specific element of the claim is verified against the primary peer-reviewed source (Gervain et al., "Adult-like processing of time-compressed speech by newborns: A NIRS study," Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience, 2017, Elsevier; PMC6987815 / PMID 27852514). Confirmed verbatim from the paper: (a) newborn mean age 2.34 days, range 1-4 days; (b) three rates tested — normal (100% duration), moderately compressed (60%, intelligible to adults), highly compressed (30%, unintelligible to adults); (c) normal and 60%-compressed speech both elicited a canonical hemodynamic response in left temporoparietal, right frontal, and right temporal cortex, while 30%-compressed produced an inverted response (decreased oxyhemoglobin); (d) pairwise comparison showed "no significant difference between normal and 60% compressed speech," supporting the claim's "no significant channel-by-channel difference"; (e) the paper's own title and conclusion state these results "mirror those found in adults" and that "adaptation to time-compressed speech requires little or no experience with speech," directly supporting "adult-like adaptation to moderate compression is present at birth." No contradicting source was found; the paper's title literally asserts "adult-like processing... by newborns." Source is primary, peer-reviewed, and of sufficient strength for the claim. Minor nuance (not refuting): the source describes the canonical responses as "weak but canonical," so the claim's "same canonical response" is a faithful but slightly hardened paraphrase of "similar"/"mirror" — within the paper's own framing.

The underlying data point is REAL and accurately transcribed: PubMed (17905900) and the ASHA/JSLHR 2007 study (Jenstad & Souza, "Temporal Envelope Changes of Compression and Speech Rate") confirm young-old (62-74, n=11) vs old-old (75-88, n=14), tested at normal/50%-time-compressed/time-restored rates, with "no difference in performance between age groups, or any interactions involving age" and "For this sample tested, the effect was not age related within the age range tested here." So checklist item 1 (quote support) largely passes. HOWEVER the claim is refuted on two grounds. (a) OVERREACH in interpretation: the claim's gloss "indicating no further decline across this older age band" generalizes a tiny-n (11 vs 14) secondary null result into a substantive aging conclusion that the study's own authors explicitly hedged ("For this sample tested... within the age range tested here"). Absence of a significant difference in n=25 hearing-impaired listeners is not evidence of no decline; it is an underpowered null. (b) CONTRADICTED by the dominant literature: Gordon-Salant & Fitzgibbons (JSLHR 2001, 1993/1995), Wingfield, the 2022 Frontiers cochlear-implant study (PMC9557069), and the 2023 hearing-loss/cortical-network study (PMC10233765) consistently report a CONTINUOUS age-related decline in time-compressed/fast-speech recognition with the OLDEST groups declining MOST. The claim's framing implies a plateau across 62-88 that the broader, larger, better-powered evidence rejects. Minor additional issue: the claim labels the materials "low-predictability sentences," which I could not independently confirm from the verified abstract text (it describes WDRC-compressed sentence recognition; high/low predictability not stated in the retrieved abstract), a possible embellishment.
result.counterSource: Gordon-Salant & Fitzgibbons, "Sources of Age-Related Recognition Difficulty for Time-Compressed Speech," JSLHR 2001 (https://pubs.asha.org/doi/10.1044/1092-4388(2001/056)); Frontiers Aging Neurosci 2022 (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9557069/) and PMC10233765 — all showing continuous age-related decline in time-compressed speech with oldest groups worst, contradicting "no further decline across this older age band."

result.evidence: The primary source (PMC6987815, peer-reviewed fNIRS study of newborns) directly supports every component of the claim. (1) Demographics match exactly: "mean age 2.34 days, range 1-4 days," healthy full-term neonates. (2) Compression rates match: 100% (normal), 60% (moderately compressed), 30% (highly compressed) of original duration. (3) Findings match: the paper states "responses to normal and moderately compressed speech are similar, showing a canonical hemodynamic response in the left temporoparietal, right frontal and right temporal cortex, while responses to highly compressed speech are inverted." (4) The channel-by-channel claim is verified: there was "no significant difference between normal and 60% compressed speech," whereas a significant difference appeared between 60% and 30% (channel 10, p=0.011). (5) The "adult-like at birth" interpretation is the authors' own: results "mirror those found in adults, who readily adapt to moderately compressed, but not to highly compressed speech," and "adaptation to time-compressed speech requires little or no experience with speech." Source quality (peer-reviewed primary neuroscience article) matches the claim's strength; not outdated, not marketing. The only minor caveat is that the normal-vs-60% similarity rests on a null result (non-significant difference), but the claim faithfully reports this as the original authors framed it without overstatement, so it does not warrant refutation.

Claim is well-supported by the primary source and accurately represents it. The paper (PMC6987815 = Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience, PubMed PMID 27852514, ScienceDirect S1878929316300469) is literally titled "Adult-like processing of time-compressed speech by newborns: A NIRS study." Verified against the article: (a) Participants were full-term neonates, mean age 2.34 days, range 1-4 days (21 retained for analysis of 59 enrolled) — exact match to the claim. (b) Conditions were normal (100%), moderately compressed (60% of original duration; intelligible to adults), and highly compressed (30%; unintelligible to adults) — matches "60% of normal duration." (c) Finding: "no significant difference between normal and 60% compressed speech," with both producing a "canonical hemodynamic response in the left temporoparietal, right frontal and right temporal cortex," while highly compressed (30%) speech produced an INVERTED response (oxyhemoglobin decrease). This supports "no significant channel-by-channel difference" between normal and moderate. (d) The "adult-like" interpretation is the authors' own — they state the pattern "resembles the intelligibility performance of adult listeners" and that "adaptation to time-compressed speech requires little or no experience with speech," indicating sound-based/auditory (not top-down linguistic) adaptation. So the claimant is not overreaching; they are quoting the authors' framing. Source quality (peer-reviewed primary fNIRS/NIRS study, NIRx NIRScout) matches claim strength. Adversarial WebSearch found no critique, contradiction, or failed replication. Only minor nuance (not refuting): "adaptation" here is inferred from equivalent neural responses to normal vs. moderate compression measured via NIRS, not from a behavioral intelligibility/comprehension measure — but the claim correctly scopes itself to "hemodynamic brain response," so this is faithful.

Source PMC6987815 is Gervain et al. "Adult-like processing of time-compressed speech by newborns: A NIRS study" (Dev Cogn Neurosci, 2016; PubMed 27852514; n=21 newborns analyzed, mean age 2.34 days). The supporting quote is verbatim-accurate: the paper does conclude adaptation "requires little or no experience with speech, and happens at an auditory, and not at a more abstract linguistic level." So the claim's two weaker components — "does not require POSTNATAL speech experience" and "operates at an auditory rather than abstract linguistic level" — are well-supported. HOWEVER, the claim's headline assertion that "the basic machinery ... is INNATE and present FROM BIRTH" is an overreach for three reasons. (1) The authors' wording is deliberately hedged: "little OR NO experience" — they explicitly do NOT commit to "innate"/experience-independent; the claim collapses this hedge into a definitive "innate." On re-fetch the source confirms "the researchers do not claim the ability is entirely innate." (2) The article and surrounding literature attribute newborn speech-processing largely to PRENATAL/intrauterine experience (audition is operational from ~2nd half of gestation; speech prosody/rhythm is transmitted in utero; gestational age correlates with neural speech discrimination). Prenatal exposure is still experience — so "present at birth" ≠ "innate/experience-independent," a conflation the claim makes. (3) It is a single small NIRS study measuring hemodynamic DISCRIMINATION (canonical vs. inverted response), not actual comprehension of fast speech, so extrapolating to "the basic machinery for fast/time-compressed speech processing is innate" overstates the evidence base. Net: quote supports the auditory-level point but not the strong innateness claim, which misreads a hedged finding and ignores the prenatal-experience mechanism the authors themselves invoke.
result.counterSource: Gervain et al. 2016 abstract itself ("little OR no experience" hedge; PubMed 27852514) plus literature on prenatal prosody exposure: "The duration of intrauterine development influences discrimination of speech prosody in infants" (PMC11475226) and Frontiers "Does Prenatal Language Experience Shape the Neonate Neural Response to Speech?" (doi 10.3389/fpsyg.2011.00222), both showing newborn speech processing depends on prenatal/intrauterine auditory experience rather than being purely innate.

result.evidence: The cited source (DOI 10.1007/s12144-021-01540-x, CrossRef-verified) is Lewandowska et al., "Development of central auditory processes in Polish children and adolescents at the age from 7 to 16 years" (Current Psychology, 2021/2023). Its sample is children/adolescents aged 7-16 plus 20 young adults aged 18-24 — there are NO older adults; the oldest participant is 24. The claim invokes "presbycusis-type factors," but presbycusis is age-related hearing loss in older adults and cannot be a confound in a 7-24 sample, making the framing a category error. The study also does NOT use time-compressed/accelerated speech; it uses dichotic digits, duration/frequency pattern, gap-detection, and speech-in-noise tests, and its "age-related differences" are DEVELOPMENTAL maturation, not the AGING decline the claim implies. The literal audiometric statement (normal hearing, pure tones <=15 dB HL 250-8000 Hz, type A tympanograms) IS supported by the quote, but the inference that this 'isolates central vs presbycusis-type factors in age-related speed-listening' is unsupported: the design has no aging dimension and no fast/compressed-speech task, so the quote cannot bear the weight of the claim as framed for this research question.
result.counterSource: CrossRef metadata for 10.1007/s12144-021-01540-x: Lewandowska M., Milner R., Ganc M., Włodarczyk E., Dołżycka J., Skarżyński H., "Development of central auditory processes in Polish children and adolescents at the age from 7 to 16 years," Current Psychology — participants aged 7-16 plus adults 18-24; tests = DDT, DPT, FPT, GDT, adaptive speech-in-noise; no older adults; no time-compressed speech.

result.evidence:
REFUTED on two grounds: overreach beyond the source AND direct contradiction by the foundational literature.

SOURCE IDENTITY: PMC6987815 is Issard & Gervain (2016), "Adult-like processing of time-compressed speech by newborns: A NIRS study." The supporting quote ("adaptation...requires little or no experience with speech, and happens at an auditory, and not at a more abstract linguistic level") is from this newborn study's abstract.

(1) "INNATE AND PRESENT FROM BIRTH" IS UNSUPPORTED OVERREACH. The source itself attributes the newborn ability to PRENATAL/intrauterine acoustic exposure (the fetus hears low-pass-filtered prosody in utero) and to experience "with sounds in general," NOT to innateness. The authors do not claim the machinery is innate; they say it needs little/no experience with SPEECH specifically, which is the opposite of "innate" — it's prenatally learned. The quote contains no words about "innate" or "from birth." The claim's "implying...innate and present from birth" is an inference the paper does not license.

(2) "AUDITORY RATHER THAN LINGUISTIC LEVEL" IS DIRECTLY CONTRADICTED by the foundational adult literature this field rests on. Sebastián-Gallés, Dupoux, Costa & Mehler (2000), "Adaptation to time-compressed speech: Phonological determinants," and Pallier et al. cross-linguistic study concluded adaptation occurs at a PHONOLOGICAL level (a linguistic level) and is LANGUAGE-SPECIFIC — transferring between phonetically similar languages (Spanish/Catalan) but not arbitrary ones. Banai & Lavner (2012, PLOS One PMC3467231) found extended training yields stimulus-specific, suprasegmental learning that does NOT generalize. So credible primary sources directly dispute the "auditory, not linguistic" generalization.

(3) WEAK EVIDENTIARY BASE: the newborn finding rests on ONE small NIRS study with a 64% data-rejection rate (only 21 of 59 newborns retained), no neural source localization, and a design the authors admit "wasn't optimized to test adaptation." Insufficient for a strong "innate human machinery" claim.
result.counterSource: Sebastián-Gallés, Dupoux, Costa & Mehler (2000), "Adaptation to time-compressed speech: Phonological determinants," Perception & Psychophysics (PubMed 10883588 / doi 10.3758/BF03206926) — concludes adaptation is at a phonological (linguistic) level and is language-specific; plus Banai & Lavner (2012) PLOS One PMC3467231 showing non-generalizing suprasegmental learning. Also the source itself, Issard & Gervain 2016 (PMC6987815 / PubMed 27852514), which attributes newborn ability to prenatal acoustic exposure, not innateness.

result.evidence: The claim overreaches the source and is contradicted by adjacent literature. SOURCE: Gervain et al., "Adult-like processing of time-compressed speech by newborns: A NIRS study" (PMC6987815 / PubMed 27852514), an NIRS study of 59 full-term newborns (mean age 2.34 days). The supporting quote is accurate, but the claim's CONCLUSION ("innate and present from birth") is not what the authors say. (1) The word "innate" appears NOWHERE in the abstract or conclusions; the abstract explicitly frames experience-dependence as an OPEN question: "Does this ability depend on experience with speech (if yes, as heard in utero or as heard postnatally), with sounds in general or is it experience-independent?" (2) The Discussion attributes newborn performance to PRENATAL experience (third-trimester fetal hearing; low-frequency prosody transmitted through maternal tissue; newborns "may have relied on their knowledge of the rhythmic structure of their mother tongue") and to "a shared labour between learning skills set by a finely-tuned ontogenetic programme, and auditory and linguistic cues available in the foetal environment." That is experience-dependent, not innate — the opposite of the claim. (3) "Little or no experience with speech" is a hedged statement about the small amount of POSTNATAL experience in 1-4-day-olds, not a positive assertion of innateness. (4) The "auditory, not linguistic level" portion is qualified/contradicted by the adult perceptual-learning literature: Sebastian-Galles/Dupoux/Mehler ("Adaptation to time-compressed speech: Phonological determinants") and Banai & Lavner conclude adult adaptation is LANGUAGE-SPECIFIC and occurs at the PHONOLOGICAL (a linguistic) level, transferring across phonologically similar languages but not freely; the newborn study itself hedges to "auditory/phonological." Thus the claim's inference "implying the basic machinery... is innate and present from birth" is an unsupported logical leap the authors did not make, and the "auditory not linguistic" framing is disputed.
result.counterSource: Gervain et al. result.evidence: The verbatim quote ("At the age of 7 there was lower DPT and FPT scores than in all participants ≥13") is accurately drawn from the abstract of Senderski et al., Current Psychology, DOI 10.1007/s12144-021-01540-x (Polish children/adolescents 7-16 + adults). The same study's detailed statistics show only the 7-year-olds differed significantly from adults on DPT, and only 7- and 8-year-olds differed on FPT; ages 9+ showed NO significant difference from adults on either test. Thus adult-like performance is reached by ~age 8-9, not 13. The "≥13" in the quote merely denotes the set of older comparison bands the (lowest-performing) 7-year-olds were contrasted against; it does NOT identify 13 as the maturation point. Independent normative literature corroborates the earlier maturation: "at 9 years of age children already present results similar to those of adults," with FPT normative scores of 40%/65%/72%/75% correct at ages 8/9/10/11 (adults >70%). So the claim conflates "7-year-olds score below the 13+ group" with "ability does not mature until ~13," an inference contradicted by the source's own per-age statistics and by the wider literature. The factual half (7yo < 13+) is supported; the load-bearing conclusion (~13 maturation) is not.

result.evidence: The supporting quote is genuine: Senderski et al., "Development of central auditory processes in Polish children and adolescents at the age from 7 to 16 years" (Current Psychology, 2021, the cited Springer article) does report 7-year-olds scoring lower than 13-year-olds on DPT (60.5% ±16) and FPT (52.9% ±17.6). HOWEVER the claim's strong conclusion — that temporal-pattern processing "is not adult-like until early adolescence (~13 years)" — is an overreach not supported by the quote and contradicted by both the same study and the broader literature. (1) LOGICAL OVERREACH: "7-year-olds lower than ≥13" does not establish 13 as the maturation point. The same article's finer data shows deficits were already resolving by ~10-12: "a trend towards lowered scores at the age of 7 compared with the age of 10-11," and after correction for multiple comparisons the differences were against ages "above 10 (or above 12)." The "≥13" cutoff is an artifact of which surviving contrast the quote happened to cite, not evidence that 10-12-year-olds are non-adult-like. (2) CONTRADICTED BY CONSENSUS: peer-reviewed reviews place maturation of auditory temporal/pattern processing at ~10-11 years ("overall maturation of temporal processing skills is reached by 10-11 years of age"), and the Duration Pattern Sequence test is clinically normed "for ages 11 and up... children over the age of eleven would test as well (or nearly as well) as adults." (3) DIRECTLY UNDERMINES THE FAST-SPEECH BRIDGE: the claim asserts this processing is "relevant to fast-speech decoding," yet time-compressed speech comprehension specifically matures EARLIER, at "8-9 years of age" (journalofhearingscience.com maturation review) — so even granting the DPT/FPT data, the inference to "fast-speech not adult-like until 13" is contradicted by the actual time-compressed-speech literature. The quote is real but the claim attaches a maturation-age conclusion (~13) and a fast-speech causal link that the source does not make and the field rejects.

result.evidence:
The quote is accurate to the source, but the CLAIM built on it is a severe overreach that misrepresents the study's scope. The DOI 10.1007/s12144-021-01540-x is "Development of central auditory processes in Polish children and adolescents at the age from 7 to 16 years" (Current Psychology, 2021).

FATAL PROBLEM 1 — Wrong population / no aging data: The study's sample is 180 children/adolescents aged 7-16 plus 20 young adults aged 18-24. It contains NO older adults (no 50s-80s), so its hearing-control protocol cannot license any conclusion that "age-related differences reflect central rather than peripheral (presbycusis-type) factors." Presbycusis is irrelevant in a 7-24 year-old sample — children and young adults do not have age-related peripheral hearing loss to control for. Controlling audibility in young people says nothing about whether the age penalty in OLDER adults is central vs peripheral.

FATAL PROBLEM 2 — No time-compressed/fast speech: The test battery was the Dichotic Digit Test, Duration Pattern Test, Frequency Pattern Test, Gap Detection Test, and adaptive Speech-in-Noise. There is NO time-compressed/accelerated speech test. The claim is being deployed in a research question about speed-listening/time-compressed speech comprehension, which this study does not measure.

FATAL PROBLEM 3 — Overstated inference: Even within its own domain, the claim "so the observed age-related differences reflect central rather than peripheral factors" overstates. Age effects were found only for DDT, DPT, and FPT (developmental maturation in children), not a presbycusis dissociation. Normal audiometry (≤15 dB HL 250-8000 Hz, type A tympanograms) was an inclusion criterion to ensure normal hearing in young participants, not a designed manipulation isolating central from peripheral aging.

The supporting quote is real, but it is cherry-picked: it is a routine inclusion criterion from a child-development study and provides zero support for the central-vs-presbycusis claim about time-compressed speech across the aging lifespan.

result.evidence: The primary source (Journal of Hearing Science, "Effect of Age on Time-Compressed Speech Perception and Speech Perception in Noise in Normal-Hearing Individuals") directly supports the claim: 29 normal-hearing native Kannada speakers aged 15-50, TCST at 0/40/50/60% compression and SPIN at +5/0/-5 dB SNR. Stated finding: "Young adults performed better than middle-aged adults in both the TCST and SPIN tests," with the conclusion that "even in individuals with normal hearing, ageing has a deleterious effect on the performance of the central auditory processing system." Peripheral hearing was controlled (pure-tone audiometry; all normal-hearing). This is independently corroborated by a peer-reviewed Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience study (PMC9557069 / Goupell et al.) on younger (19-23), middle-aged (52-64), and older (65-78) normal-hearing listeners: time-compressed speech decline was greater for older than younger, and "the performance of the middle-aged listeners was more similar to that of the older listeners than to that of the younger listeners, especially at higher degrees of time compression," with cognitive processing speed NOT predicting the effect, implicating central auditory (not peripheral) changes. Caveat: the JHS abstract does not give explicit p-values, so the claim's word "significantly" slightly outruns the quoted text, but the direction, normal-hearing control, and central-mechanism conclusion are all directly stated and convergent across sources. Source is a peer-reviewed primary study, appropriate for the claim's strength; not marketing, not outdated, not contradicted.

Three disqualifying facts: (1) It is a DEVELOPMENTAL/maturation study of children/adolescents (180 subjects aged 7-15.9 yr) plus 20 young adults (18-24 yr) — there are NO older adults, so the claim's reference to "presbycusis-type factors" is meaningless (presbycusis is age-related hearing loss in OLDER adults; oldest participant here is 24). (2) The study does NOT use time-compressed/fast speech: methods state "the compressed speech test, used in the previous study, was replaced with the speech-in-noise." Tests used are Dichotic Digit, Duration Pattern, Frequency Pattern, Gap Detection, and adaptive Speech-in-Noise — none accelerated. (3) The "age-related differences reflect central rather than peripheral" framing is the authors' point about CANS maturation in children, not about presbycusis in aging adults. result.evidence:
PARTIAL SUPPORT, BUT OVERREACH on the load-bearing inference. The literal quote is accurate: in this study (Skoczylas et al., "Development of central auditory processes in Polish children and adolescents 7-16 years," Current Psychology 2021), 7-year-olds scored well below older groups on DPT (60.5%±16 vs 13-yr 82.1%±8) and FPT (52.9%±17.6 vs 13-yr 78.3%±9.6), and the paper states 7-year-olds scored lower than 13/14/15-year-olds and adults. So the comparison "7-yr < all ≥13" is real.

HOWEVER the claim's headline inference — "central temporal processing... not adult-like until early adolescence (~13 years)" — overreaches the DPT/FPT evidence three ways:
(1) The study's OWN DPT/FPT means plateau by ~9-10 yrs, not 13: DPT is 77% at age 9, 81.5% at 10, ~80-81% through 12 (adult 88.3%); FPT is 66.5% at 9, ~71-73% at 10-12. After Bonferroni correction, 10-, 11-, and 12-year-olds did NOT differ significantly from adults on DPT/FPT; only the 9-yr DPT showed a trend. The study's "completed until 13 years" conclusion is driven mainly by the LEFT-EAR DICHOTIC DIGIT (DDT) test, not by DPT/FPT. So tying the ~13 maturation specifically to DPT/FPT misreads which test produced that boundary.
(2) The "~13" figure is population-specific — authors note some findings are "specific for the Polish population," and broader literature places FPT/DPT temporal-ordering maturation at ~10-12 yrs (corpus callosum adult-level ~10-11 yrs), contradicting the 13-yr framing.
(3) "Relevant to fast-speech decoding" is unsupported by the source: the authors explicitly REPLACED their prior compressed/time-compressed speech test with speech-in-noise and draw NO link between DPT/FPT and fast/time-compressed speech comprehension. The claim's relevance hook to the research question (speed-listening) is the claim-writer's extrapolation, not the study's finding.

Net: the quoted comparison is true, but the strong conclusion ("not adult-like until ~13" + "relevant to fast-speech decoding") is a cherry-pick/overreach of the DPT/FPT data, which actually mature ~10, and is unconnected to fast speech in the source.

result.evidence: The claim accurately restates the source. WebFetch of the primary article (Journal of Hearing Science) confirms: (a) all participants were normal-hearing; (b) the study spanned ages 15-50 (29 native Kannada speakers); (c) it found younger adults (15-30) outperformed middle-aged (40-50) on both time-compressed and speech-in-noise tasks despite normal thresholds; and (d) the verbatim conclusion is "Even in individuals with normal hearing, ageing has a deleterious effect on the performance of the central auditory processing system, which is necessary for speech perception under adverse listening conditions." The supporting quote is genuine and the claim does not overreach the study's own framing — attributing decline to central (not peripheral) processing is exactly the authors' inference, justified by the normal-hearing design that holds audibility roughly constant. Independent peer-reviewed literature corroborates the central-processing attribution for normal-hearing aging listeners (e.g., PMC4906303 'Overview of Central Auditory Processing Deficits in Older Adults'; PMC9557069 Frontiers time-compressed-speech study finding age x compression interaction with reduced temporal resolution 'independent of peripheral hearing loss'). Two minor qualifications that do NOT rise to refutation: (1) the study tops out at age 50, so it does not directly speak to the research question's 50s-80s bands; (2) the broader literature frames central factors as 'at least partially' responsible alongside interacting peripheral/cognitive factors, slightly softer than a pure central-only attribution. Neither contradicts the claim as scoped to this specific study. Source quality is adequate: a peer-reviewed journal article making a non-extraordinary, mechanistically standard claim that aligns with mainstream audiology consensus.

result.evidence: The claim is well-supported by the primary source and corroborated by independent literature. The Journal of Hearing Science study (peer-reviewed, native Kannada speakers, n=29, ages 15-50, "young and middle-aged adults" with normal hearing) concludes verbatim: "ageing has a deleterious effect on the performance of the central auditory processing system, which is necessary for speech perception under adverse listening conditions." Because participants were normal-hearing yet young adults outperformed middle-aged adults on both time-compressed speech (TCST) and speech-in-noise (SPIN) tests, the central-not-peripheral attribution is the study's own stated reasoning, not an overreach. Independent corroboration: a controlled study (PMC9557069) found "deficits in speech recognition of older listeners for time-compressed speech may be primarily affected by age-related declines in central auditory processing rather than solely related to peripheral age-related hearing loss," and that cognitive processing speed did NOT predict time-compression effects, isolating central auditory (not general cognitive) factors. No credible source contradicts this; it reflects the mainstream audiology consensus (Gordon-Salant, Wingfield lineage). CAVEAT for downstream use (does not refute the claim as worded): this specific study's "age" range was only 15-50, so its "age-related decline" is a young-vs-middle-aged contrast, NOT a 50s-80s older-adult cohort; and the fetched page did not specify the exact audiometric cutoff (dB HL/frequency range) used to define "normal hearing," so the degree to which audibility was controlled is not fully verifiable from the abstract alone.

result.evidence: Claim is well-supported and corroborated. PRIMARY SOURCE (Kumar, Pradhan, Handa & Sanju, Journal of Hearing Science, 2016; AIISH): n=29 normal-hearing native Kannada speakers aged 15-50; TCST at 0/40/50/60% compression and SPIN at +5/0/-5 dB SNR. This directly matches the claim's direction, population, and normal-hearing condition. The claim accurately reports the study's own conclusion and is directly backed by the supporting quote. The cited Journal of Hearing Science paper (peer-reviewed) studied 29 normal-hearing native Kannada speakers aged 15-50 and concluded verbatim: "Even in individuals with normal hearing, ageing has a deleterious effect on the performance of the central auditory processing system, which is necessary for speech perception under adverse listening conditions." Young adults outperformed middle-aged adults on both time-compressed speech and speech-in-noise despite normal audiograms, and the age x compression interaction grew at higher compression. Convergent peer-reviewed evidence supports a genuine central contribution independent of peripheral hearing: cochlear-implant time-compression studies (PMC9557069) isolate central effects by bypassing cochlear encoding, and temporal-processing deficits predict word-recognition decline even after adjusting for age, hearing level, and cognition (PMC5587455). QUALIFICATIONS (not refutations): (a) The claim is a faithful "the study attributes..." reporting statement, not an assertion that central processing is the sole/proven cause. (b) Broader reviews caution the central-vs-peripheral separation is unresolved — the AAA 2012 position notes "many studies are confounded by peripheral presbycusis, cognitive decline, or both," and whether a "pure" central deficit exists is "less clear" (PMC4906303); cognitive/working-memory factors co-contribute. (c) Methodological flag: standard "normal-hearing" audiometric screening (typically ≤25 dB HL) does not exclude extended-high-frequency loss or suprathreshold cochlear synaptopathy, so "normal-hearing therefore central not peripheral" is a logically imperfect inference; and the 15-50 sample does not cover older adults (60s-80s). None of these contradict the claim as stated — they qualify its strength.

The reported figures match the source table exactly: 60% compression older 0.537 vs younger 0.740, t(49)=4.859, p<0.001; 50% compression older 0.795 vs younger 0.868, t(49)=2.725, p=0.009; 40% compression no significant age difference (t=1.634, p=0.109). VERIFIED against primary source PMC6364265 (Brown et al., "Time-Compressed Speech Identification Is Predicted by Auditory Neural Processing, Perceptuomotor Speed, and Executive Functioning in Younger and Older Listeners," JARO 2019; N=51: 21 younger 19-30, 30 older 56-82; task = R-SPIN sentence final-word identification at 40/50/60% compression). The age effect was instead independently predicted/mediated by P1 latency (auditory neural processing), perceptuomotor processing speed, and executive functioning. No overreach: the claim is precisely scoped to a normal-hearing sample and accurately reports the null mediation result. CAVEAT (not a refutation): the sample is normal-hearing-only with restricted audiometric range, and older adults still had significantly higher thresholds than younger; thus this null does not generalize to hearing-impaired populations — and a related study (PMC10233765) finds age-related hearing loss DOES accelerate fast-speech decline in populations that include hearing loss. But that does not contradict this claim's narrow scope. Source is a peer-reviewed primary study in a reputable otolaryngology journal; quality matches the claim's strength; not outdated for this slow-moving topic; not a marketing/press claim.
