result.evidence: The claim is well-supported by the primary peer-reviewed source (Dias, McClaskey & Harris, "Time-Compressed Speech Identification Is Predicted by Auditory Neural Processing, Perceptuomotor Speed, and Executive Functioning in Younger and Older Listeners," JARO 2018, 20(1):73-88; PMC6364265). Direct verification: (1) The study states the age-group effect, "a significant predictor of time-compressed speech identification in model 1a, became a nonsignificant predictor in model 2a," and that "the addition of perceptuomotor processing speed in model 2a mediated the effect of age group" — matching the supporting quote and the claim's core assertion. (2) Hearing was controlled: participants had audiometric thresholds <=25 dB HL (250-3000 Hz), and the authors explicitly found audiometric thresholds did NOT mediate the age effect, strengthening the central-vs-peripheral framing. (3) Sample: 21 younger (19-30) and 30 older (56-82) adults; perceptuomotor speed measured via the Connections Test (simple condition). MINOR OVERREACH/CAVEAT (not enough to refute): the word "drives" overstates a single-factor account — the same study found auditory neural processing (P1/N1 latencies) and executive functioning ALSO independently predicted performance, so perceptuomotor speed is one of several contributors, not the sole driver. Also, the Connections Test is a visual-motor paper-pencil task (the authors flag it as "visual-based"), so labeling it cleanly "central processing speed" is a simplification. A web search surfaced a contrasting study (Frontiers/PMC9557069, time-compressed speech in CI/NH listeners) where a cognitive processing speed measure did NOT predict time-compression effects, suggesting central auditory changes — but that is a different study with a different measure and qualifies the broader literature rather than contradicting what THIS study reported. Since the claim is framed as reporting this specific study's mediation finding, it is accurate. Source quality (primary, peer-reviewed, hearing-controlled) matches the claim's strength.

result.evidence: CONFIRMED by primary source (Dias, McClaskey & Harris 2018, "Time-Compressed Speech Identification Is Predicted by Auditory Neural Processing, Perceptuomotor Speed, and Executive Functioning in Younger and Older Listeners," JARO; PMC6364265). The source directly verifies all four specific data points: (a) audiometric inclusion criterion of thresholds ≤25 dB HL between 250-3000 Hz; (b) adding audiometric averages to the model "did not significantly improve model fit," χ²(1)=0.130, p=0.718; (c) the right-ear threshold predictor was "not a significant predictor," t(48)=0.350, p=0.728; (d) paper concludes "variability in time-compressed speech identification accounted for by age group is not explained by individual differences in audiometric thresholds." Sample: 21 younger (19-30) vs 30 older (56-82) adults. The age effect was instead mediated by perceptuomotor processing speed (a central factor), exactly matching the claim's framing. CONVERGENT independent evidence: Füllgrabe et al. 2014 (Front. Aging Neurosci., PMC4292733) found the same pattern — age-group speech-identification deficits persist despite matched audiometrically normal hearing, attributed to central temporal processing and cognition rather than peripheral audibility. No contradicting source found. Minor caveat: the claim's supporting quote ("audiometric thresholds do not mediate...") is a faithful paraphrase, not the paper's verbatim wording, but does not misrepresent the finding.

CLAIM SUPPORTED by the primary peer-reviewed source (Dias, McClaskey & Harris, 2018/2019, "Time-Compressed Speech Identification Is Predicted by Auditory Neural Processing, Perceptuomotor Speed, and Executive Functioning in Younger and Older Listeners," J Assoc Res Otolaryngol; PMC6364265). The paper's own wording matches the claim: age group was a significant predictor of time-compressed speech identification in model 1a but "became a nonsignificant predictor" once perceptuomotor processing speed was added; the abstract states "declines in perceptuomotor processing speed mediated age-group differences in time-compressed speech identification," and the Discussion states "age-group differences...are mediated by individual differences in perceptuomotor processing speed." Sample: younger N=21 (19-30), older N=30 (56-82). The "central not peripheral" angle is reinforced because audiometric thresholds were tested and did NOT mediate the age effect. QUALIFICATIONS (not refutations): (a) The authors used nested-model coefficient comparison, not a formal mediation test (no Sobel/bootstrapped indirect effect), so "statistically mediated" is the authors' own term but methodologically informal. (b) The claim's phrase "central processing speed rather than age per se drives the deficit" slightly overstates: processing speed was one of THREE independent predictors (also P1/auditory neural latency and executive functioning), and the authors elsewhere hedge "may be partially accounted for." (c) Small N. (d) A separate 2022 cochlear-implant/normal-hearing study (PMC9557069) found a general cognitive processing-speed measure did NOT predict time-compression effects, showing the processing-speed account is not universally replicated — but this does not contradict the specific perceptuomotor finding in PMC6364265. None of these reach the level of refutation; the claim is accurately drawn from its cited primary source.

result.evidence: CONFIRMED, NOT REFUTED. Primary source verified: Dias, McClaskey & Harris, "Time-Compressed Speech Identification Is Predicted by Auditory Neural Processing, Perceptuomotor Speed, and Executive Functioning in Younger and Older Listeners," J Assoc Res Otolaryngol (JARO), 2018/2019 (PMC6364265 / PubMed 30456729). The abstract states verbatim: "declines in perceptuomotor processing speed mediated age-group differences in time-compressed speech identification." Regression confirms TRUE mediation: in model 1a, age group was a significant predictor of time-compressed speech identification; the model that ADDED perceptuomotor processing speed (Connections Simple test) is the SAME model in which age group "became a nonsignificant predictor" — not a case where age was already nonsignificant. Audibility was controlled: listeners were normal-hearing (thresholds <=25 dB HL, 250-3000 Hz) and audiometric thresholds did not significantly predict performance, so the age penalty persists after controlling for audibility and is attributed to central processing speed. The claim faithfully reproduces the supporting quote and the authors' own conclusion that central processing speed rather than chronological age drives the deficit. Source quality (peer-reviewed primary study in a reputable hearing-science journal) matches the claim's strength; it is current (2018/2019) and is not a press release or benchmark. Minor scoping note (does not refute): the paper found P1 latency and executive functioning ALSO independently predict performance, but perceptuomotor speed is specifically the mediator of the age-group effect, which is exactly what the claim asserts.

Claim is fully supported by the primary source (Dias, McClaskey & Harris, 2018, "Time-Compressed Speech Identification Is Predicted by Auditory Neural Processing, Perceptuomotor Speed, and Executive Functioning in Younger and Older Listeners," PMC6364265, a peer-reviewed JARO article). Table 5 / Model 4 (combined model) confirms all three standardized betas EXACTLY as claimed: P1 peak latency beta=-0.181 (p=0.004), perceptuomotor processing speed beta=0.242 (p=0.004), executive functioning beta=0.132 (p=0.031). All three remained significant SIMULTANEOUSLY in the same model after accounting for age group, compression rate, and their interaction. Direction is correctly interpreted: longer P1 latency = worse identification (negative beta). Audibility was controlled — sample was normal-hearing (thresholds <=25 dB HL, 250-3000 Hz), 21 younger (19-30) and 30 older (56-82) adults — which directly addresses the research question's requirement to flag whether peripheral hearing is controlled. Source quality matches claim strength; not marketing/forum/press release; 2018 is current for this slow-moving psychoacoustics field.

The claim is directly and verbatim supported by the primary peer-reviewed source (Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience, 2022, doi:10.3389/fnagi.2022.887581). Verified via WebFetch of the full article: (1) Compression levels 0%, 20%, 40%, 60% — confirmed. (2) Syllables/sec figures match exactly: the paper states "At 0% time compression, the talker spoke at an average rate of approximately 3.7 syllables per second. The rate increased to approximately 4.6 syllables per second in the 20% time-compressed sentences, approximately 6.4 syllables per second in the 40% time-compressed sentences, and approximately 10 syllables per second in the 60% time-compressed sentences." (3) Ceiling/decline pattern matches verbatim: "Performance was at ceiling for unprocessed speech in the 0 and 20% time-compressed conditions and declined beginning at 40% time compression." Source quality is sufficient: peer-reviewed primary research, not a press release or benchmark. The claim is not outdated (2022) and not contradicted. One minor framing caveat: the paper's ceiling sentence refers to normal-hearing (NH) listeners generally rather than exclusively "younger" NH listeners — older NH listeners declined more at higher compression — but this is a slight attribution nuance, not a misread of the numeric thresholds or the core ceiling-then-decline finding, all of which are exact. Does not rise to a refutation.

VERIFIED against the primary source (JARO 2018/2019, PMC6364265). Every statistic in the claim matches Table 5 Model 4 (the combined model) exactly: P1 peak latency standardized β=−0.181, t(46)=−3.045, p=0.004 (negative coefficient = longer latency → worse identification, as claimed); perceptuomotor processing speed β=0.242, t(46)=3.028, p=0.004; executive functioning β=0.132, t(46)=2.226, p=0.031. All three remained significant SIMULTANEOUSLY in the same combined regression model, exactly as the claim states. The supporting quote is VERBATIM from the discussion: "time-compressed speech identification is subject to both low-level peripheral processing and higher-level perceptuomotor processing speed and executive functioning ability." Source quality is sufficient: peer-reviewed Journal of the Association for Research in Otolaryngology. Sample = 21 younger (19–30y) + 30 older (56–82y) adults, all normal-hearing (≤25 dB HL, 250–3000 Hz); paper explicitly shows audiometric thresholds do NOT mediate the age effect on time-compressed speech, so the age penalty persists after controlling for audibility. P1 is described as primary-auditory-cortex (central) processing, justifying the "auditory neural processing" label. Minor caveat: the word "independent" is slightly loose — the paper found perceptuomotor speed MEDIATES the age-group effect (not fully independent of age), but the three predictors do all remain simultaneously significant in the combined model, which is precisely what the claim asserts. This nuance does not rise to the level of refutation. No contradicting evidence found; claim is accurate, current for this study, and directly relevant to the age/mechanism angles (peripheral vs central/cognitive) of the research question.

Claim verified against the primary source PMC6364265 (Gordon-Salant-related Frontiers study, "Time-Compressed Speech Identification Is Predicted by Auditory Neural Processing, Perceptuomotor Speed, and Executive Functioning in Younger and Older Listeners"). All three statistics match exactly: P1 peak latency beta=-0.181, p=0.004 (longer latency = worse); perceptuomotor processing speed (Connections Simple) beta=0.242, p=0.004; executive functioning beta=0.132, p=0.031. The "all remaining significant simultaneously" portion is confirmed: in the combined Model 4, the article states "time compression rate, P1 peak latency, Connections Simple scores, executive functioning, and the interaction between age group and time compression rate were all found to significantly predict time-compressed speech identification" — i.e., all three retained as significant in the SAME model, not separate models. Sample: 21 younger (19-30) + 30 older (56-82) adults. Audibility WAS controlled (thresholds <=25 dB HL 250-3000 Hz, stimuli at 90 dB SPL in quiet), and the age penalty persisted via the age-group x compression-rate interaction — directly relevant to mechanism angle #3. WebSearch found no contradicting source; corroborating PMC articles confirm. Source is peer-reviewed primary, on-topic, current. Minor caveat: the supporting quote calls P1 "peripheral," but the study frames P1 as central/neural encoding; the claim text itself correctly labels it "auditory neural processing," so the claim is accurate.

Both components of the claim are verbatim-confirmed from the primary source (Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience, 2022, doi:10.3389/fnagi.2022.887581). (1) The ceiling/decline statement is an exact quote: "Performance was at ceiling for unprocessed speech in the 0 and 20% time-compressed conditions and declined beginning at 40% time compression." (2) The syllable rates are exact: Materials and Methods states the talker spoke at ~3.7 syll/s (0%), ~4.6 syll/s (20%), ~6.4 syll/s (40%), and ~10 syll/s (60%). The study tested younger normal-hearing listeners (YNH, n=15, ages 19-23), so the claim's attribution to "younger normal-hearing listeners" is valid — they are included in and exhibit the described pattern. ONE MINOR IMPRECISION (not refuting): the ceiling-at-0%/20% statement actually applies to ALL three normal-hearing age groups (YNH, MNH, ONH) for unprocessed speech, not exclusively the younger group; age differences emerge at 40-60% compression where MNH/ONH decline more steeply ("The decline in performance for the middle-aged and older listeners appears to be larger than the decline... for YNH listeners"). This narrows the attribution slightly but does not contradict the claim. Source is a peer-reviewed primary research article, current (2022), appropriate to claim strength; not marketing/press-release/cherry-pick. No contradicting evidence found.

All three sub-claims are directly supported by the primary peer-reviewed source (Goupell-related study in Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience 2022, also on PubMed PMID 36247992 / PMC9557069). (1) "Before 65": Middle-aged normal-hearing group was 52-64 yrs (M=59.75), all under 65; paper states age effects "may affect performance by adults prior to 65 years of age" and "as young as 50 years old (or younger)." (2) "Middle-aged and older showed similar deficits vs younger": Verbatim — "There were no significant differences in performance between the MNH and ONH groups for 40 and 60% time-compressed speech or overall (all p's > 0.05)," with both groups significantly worse than younger listeners. (3) "Largest declines at 40-60% compression": Study tested 0/20/40/60%; significant age x compression interactions were "driven by performance differences at 40 and 60% time compression between the YNH group and both the MNH and ONH groups." Hearing was controlled (NH ≤25 dB HL, 250-4000 Hz) and the paper attributes the deficit to central auditory processing rather than peripheral presbycusis, reinforcing the claim. One minor nuance: 60% was the maximum compression tested (no >60% rates), so "40-60%" is the largest-decline band only within the tested range — but this does not contradict the claim. No contradicting sources found; corroborating literature aligns.

CLAIM VERIFIED against primary source (Tinnemore et al. 2022, Front. Aging Neurosci. 10.3389/fnagi.2022.887581), confirmed from both Frontiers and PMC9557069 full text. (1) "Middle-aged and older showed similar deficits relative to younger": DIRECTLY CONFIRMED — "There were no significant differences in performance between the MNH [52-64 yrs, M~60] and ONH [65-78 yrs, M~70] groups for 40 and 60% time-compressed speech or overall (all p's > 0.05)"; middle-aged CI and NH performance "was consistent with that of the older listeners, rather than appearing at an intermediate range." (2) "Largest declines at 40-60% compression": DIRECTLY CONFIRMED — compression levels tested were 0/20/40/60%; significant age×compression interactions "driven by the differences in performance at 40 and 60% time compression between the YNH group and both the MNH and ONH groups." (3) "Before age 65": CONFIRMED as faithful paraphrase — authors conclude "potential age-related differences in central auditory processing may affect performance by adults prior to 65 years of age" (MNH group). Minor caveat: documented effect is within the 50s-early-60s band (range 52-64), not the 40s, so "before 65" is accurate but should not be read as "in middle age generally / 40s." Source quality matches claim strength: peer-reviewed primary research, 2022 (current). Hearing loss IS dissociated via cochlear-implant cohort that bypasses peripheral presbycusis, supporting the central-auditory interpretation. A search-surfaced study (middle-aged 42-54 affected LESS than older 60-69) does NOT contradict this — it samples a younger middle-aged band and is consistent with graded onset; the claim is explicitly scoped to this primary source, which it represents accurately.

result.evidence: Claim is well-supported by the primary source (Tinnemore, Montero, Gordon-Salant, Goupell 2022, Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience; PMID 36247992, PMC9557069), a peer-reviewed study. Verbatim quote confirms point 1: "The older and middle age groups showed similar performances, regardless of the mode of listening (acoustic or with a CI), indicating that potential age-related differences in central auditory processing may affect performance by adults prior to 65 years of age." Age bands: younger 19-23 (NH); middle-aged 52-64 (NH)/50-63 (CI); older 65-78 (NH)/65-82 (CI), so "before 65" is grounded in the 50-64 middle-aged group. Similar deficits confirmed: "no significant differences in performance between the MCI and OCI groups at either 40% time compression or 60% time compression," and middle-aged performance was "consistent with that of the older listeners, rather than appearing at an intermediate range." Compression tested at 0/20/40/60%; performance near ceiling at 0-20% and declined substantially at 40% and especially 60% (~10 syll/sec), matching "largest declines at 40-60% compression." Hearing loss controlled: NH listeners met ≤25 dB HL (250-4000 Hz) criteria and CI users bypass peripheral loss, supporting the central-processing interpretation. No contradicting sources found; an independent line (decline in speech-in-noise from ~50 years) corroborates rather than refutes. Minor nuance: paper frames 60% as most pronounced with 40% also significant, but the "40-60%" range is accurate.

The claim is well-supported by the primary source (Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience, 2022; PMC9557069), which I verified directly. The study tested 58 cochlear-implant (CI) users and 46 normal-hearing (NH) listeners across younger/middle-aged/older bands. It found (a) a significant age × time-compression interaction (greater speech-recognition decline at faster rates for older adults), and (b) NO significant listener-group × age-group interaction (all p > 0.05) — i.e., the age-related decline was statistically similar in CI and NH listeners. Since CI stimulation bypasses outer/middle/inner-ear sources of presbycusis, similar decline in both groups supports a central rather than purely peripheral source. The supporting quote ('primarily affected by age-related declines in central auditory processing rather than solely related to peripheral age-related hearing loss') is verbatim and accurately represented. Source is peer-reviewed primary research, current (2022), not marketing. Two caveats keep confidence at medium rather than high: (1) the claim hardens the authors' heavily hedged language ('may be,' 'at least partially,' 'rather than SOLELY peripheral') into a firmer attribution; (2) the authors themselves flag that CIs do NOT fully bypass peripheral aging (spiral-ganglion/auditory-nerve degeneration persists; older CI users may have had pre-implant peripheral loss; older NH listeners had worse 8 kHz thresholds), and they did not directly measure non-speech central temporal processing. These are limitations on confidence, not contradictions — the core finding (age penalty persists when peripheral cochlear encoding is bypassed; central processing is the dominant attributed factor) is correctly stated. A converging study (PMC10233765) shows hearing loss ACCELERATES fast-speech decline, which complements rather than refutes the central-processing claim. No credible source contradicts the central-attribution conclusion.

Primary source (Letowski & Poch, 1996, J Am Acad Audiol 7:447-457, PMID 8972446) directly supports the claim. Verified via PubMed and ResearchGate: 15 middle-aged (42-54 yrs) and 15 older (60-69 yrs) adults were tested on time-compressed passages across 3 compression rates (30/45/60%) and 7 discard interval lengths (35-155 msec). The abstract states verbatim: "The older adults were affected more by CR and DIL values than middle-aged adults," and concludes "time-compressed speech differentiates between speech comprehension by middle-aged and older adults." The claim is a faithful, non-overreaching restatement (it asserts a measurable age-related decline between middle and older age — exactly what the data show). No contradicting evidence found; the direction of the effect is corroborated by convergent peer-reviewed literature (Vaughan/Letowski follow-ups; ASHA 2001 "Sources of Age-Related Recognition Difficulty for Time-Compressed Speech"; 2019 PMC6364265; 2022 Frontiers PMC9557069), all showing older adults are more susceptible to time-compression, with middle-aged listeners patterning between young and old. Source is peer-reviewed primary research matching the claim's modest strength; not a benchmark/press release. Caveat: the 1996 study did not fully control for peripheral hearing loss (presbycusis), but the claim makes no causal/mechanistic assertion (only a behavioral decline), so audibility confounding does not undermine it. Age (1996) is not disqualifying for this robust, repeatedly replicated behavioral finding.

Claim is well-supported by the primary source (Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience 2022, fnagi.2022.887581 / PMC9557069), a peer-reviewed study testing 58 cochlear-implant (CI) users and 46 normal-hearing (NH) listeners across young/middle/older age bands. (1) The CI rationale in the claim is verbatim the paper's design logic: "the CI bypasses many of the outer, middle, and inner ear sources of age-related hearing loss." (2) CI users showed the SAME age pattern as NH listeners — the paper reports no significant listener-group x age-group interactions (all p>0.05), and older/middle-aged CI users were significantly worse than young CI users at 40% and 60% time compression (p<0.001 and p=0.003), despite bypassing cochlear pathology. (3) The attribution to central rather than peripheral processing matches the supporting quote exactly: "deficits...may be primarily affected by age-related declines in central auditory processing rather than solely related to peripheral age-related hearing loss." The claim's word "primarily" mirrors the paper's "primarily...rather than solely." Corroboration: cognitive processing speed (Symbol Search) did NOT predict the time-compression effect, sharpening the attribution to central AUDITORY (not general cognitive) processing. Independent WebSearch found no source disputing the central-processing attribution; the broader literature (Central Presbycusis reviews, Humes 2016, 2023 fast-speech study) is consistent — hearing loss may accelerate decline but central aging is an independent contributor. Minor hedges: the study notes older NH listeners had poorer 8kHz thresholds (a residual audibility confound it acknowledges), and the conclusion is appropriately hedged ("may be," "not solely") — but the claim itself uses matching cautious phrasing ("primarily," "bypass much"), so it is not an overreach. Source quality (peer-reviewed primary, 2022, current) matches claim strength.

VERIFIED against primary source (Letowski & Poch, 1996, "Comprehension of time-compressed speech: effects of age and speech complexity," J Am Acad Audiol 7:447-457; PMID 8972446). Two independent retrievals confirm: (a) compression rates tested were exactly 30%, 45%, 60%; (b) discard interval lengths (DIL) were 35-155 ms (7 DILs); (c) the supporting quote is VERBATIM from the abstract: "In all cases, performance of the subjects deteriorated with increasing CR and DIL." Study population: 15 middle-aged (42-54 yrs) and 15 older (60-69 yrs) adults. The claim's paraphrase ("higher compression produced worse comprehension in all subjects") accurately reflects "in all cases ... deteriorated with increasing CR" — no overreach. Source quality is adequate-to-strong: it is the cited peer-reviewed primary source, correctly matched to the PMID. The directional finding (comprehension falls as compression increases) is uncontroversial and broadly replicated. Minor caveat (does NOT refute): the abstract does not explicitly state hearing status, though it implies subjects whose deficits exceed peripheral hearing loss; this does not affect the narrow claim being made.

The claim is well-supported by the primary peer-reviewed source. Letowski & Poch (1996), "Comprehension of time-compressed speech: effects of age and speech complexity," J Am Acad Audiol 7:447-457 (PMID 8972446), studied 15 middle-aged adults (42-54 yrs) and 15 older adults (60-69 yrs) using periodic-sampling time compression with CR (compression rate: 30/45/60%) and DIL (discard interval length: 35-155 ms). The abstract explicitly states: "Performance of subjects deteriorated with increasing CR and DIL in all cases, and the older adults were affected more by CR and DIL values than middle-aged adults." This matches the claim's age bands (60-69 vs 42-54), the direction of the effect, and the conclusion of an age-related decline between middle and older age. The claim does NOT overreach: it does not assert audibility was controlled, and CR/DIL terms are correctly characterized. Independent corroborating literature confirms the same direction (Wingfield/Tun, Gordon-Salant; pubs.asha.org/doi/10.1044/1092-4388(2001/056); pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9557069). No credible source contradicts the basic finding that older adults show greater time-compressed-speech decrement than middle-aged adults. Source quality (peer-reviewed primary study) matches the modest strength of the claim. Caveat: with n=15/group and a 1996 study, effect-size magnitude is not given here, but the claim only asserts a "measurable" difference, which the source supports.

CONFIRMED, not refuted. PubMed 8972446 (Letowski & Poch, 1996, J Am Acad Audiol 7:447-457, "Comprehension of time-compressed speech: effects of age and speech complexity") was independently verified via WebFetch of the PubMed abstract AND a web search reproducing the same abstract/findings. Every specific in the claim matches the source verbatim: (a) "Three CRs (30, 45, and 60%)" = claim's "30%, 45%, and 60%"; (b) "seven DILs (35 through 155 msec)" = claim's "discard intervals of 35-155 ms"; (c) the supporting quote is real and appears in the abstract: "In all cases, performance of the subjects deteriorated with increasing CR and DIL." The claim's paraphrase "higher compression produced worse comprehension in all subjects" faithfully renders "in all cases, performance...deteriorated with increasing CR." No overreach: the study did test 15 middle-aged (42-54 yr) and 15 older (60-69 yr) adults, and the directional finding (worse comprehension at higher compression) is uncontroversial and corroborated across the broader literature. Source quality (peer-reviewed primary study) matches the claim's modest, descriptive strength. Attempted refutations (misread of quote, parameter mismatch, contradicting sources, low source quality) all failed.

The claim is directly and accurately supported by the primary source. Letowski & Poch (1996), "Comprehension of time-compressed speech: effects of age and speech complexity," J Am Acad Audiol 7(6):447-57 (PMID 8972446), studied exactly the cited age bands — 15 middle-aged (42-54 yr) and 15 older (60-69 yr) adults — and the abstract states verbatim: "the older adults were affected more by CR [compression rate] and DIL [discard interval length] values than middle-aged adults," concluding "time-compressed speech differentiates between speech comprehension by middle-aged and older adults." The claim's wording ("measurable age-related decline... between middle and older age") matches this without overreach. The finding is robustly corroborated by independent peer-reviewed literature: Wingfield & Tun, "Sources of age-related recognition difficulty for time-compressed speech," J Speech Lang Hear Res (2001, PMID 11521766); a Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience 2022 study (PMC9557069); and the 2018 Frontiers narrative review — all confirming older adults comprehend time-compressed/rapid speech more poorly than younger/middle-aged adults, with middle-aged performance intermediate. The direction is consistent across 25+ years, so the 1996 date is not a liability. CAVEAT (qualification, not refutation): the Letowski & Poch abstract does not state whether audiometric thresholds were screened/matched, so it cannot by itself establish a CENTRAL (presbycusis-independent) decline — the middle-aged vs older difference could be partly confounded by peripheral hearing loss; sample is small (n=15/group) and significance statistics are not in the abstract. But the claim as worded only asserts an age-related decline, not a central mechanism, so this does not refute it.

Primary source PMC6238820 (Lemos et al., "Ongoing maturation in the time-compressed speech test," CoDAS, Brazil, Brazilian Portuguese, n=60: 30 children aged 8-10 [mean 9±0.83] and 30 young adults aged 16-24 [mean 21.03±2.14]) at 60% compression ratio confirms every numeric claim. Verbatim: young adults "perform significantly better than children on the TCST in both the monosyllable and disyllable modalities and in both ears" with p<0.001 for all comparisons. Disyllables right ear: Children mean 39.67 (SD 2.50) vs Adults mean 46.70 (SD 2.02) — exact match. The interpretation ("still maturing during childhood/adolescence, higher in young adults") is the study's OWN thesis — its title is "Ongoing maturation in the time-compressed speech test" and it concludes "the influence and impact of age (maturational factor) on TCS test performance." WebSearch corroborated rather than contradicted (multiple sources agree young adults outperform children; cognitive maturation continues into young adulthood). Source is peer-reviewed and PubMed/PMC-indexed; claim strength (developmental maturation, descriptive normative-style comparison) matches source quality. Numbers rounded with "~" but accurate. Minor flag: study screened for normal hearing rather than statistically controlling for audibility, but the claim makes no audibility-controlled assertion, so this is not a defect in the claim as worded.

The claim is faithfully supported by the primary source: Letowski & Poch (1996), "Comprehension of time-compressed speech: effects of age and speech complexity," J Am Acad Audiol 7(6):447-57 (PMID 8972446). The abstract independently confirms every specific figure in the claim: three compression rates of 30%, 45%, and 60%; seven discard interval lengths of 35 through 155 msec; and the verbatim finding "In all cases, performance of the subjects deteriorated with increasing CR and DIL." Subjects were 30 healthy adults (15 middle-aged, 42-54 yrs; 15 older, 60-69 yrs). The directional principle (more compression = worse comprehension) is robustly replicated across the independent literature (e.g., Wingfield, Gordon-Salant & Fitzgibbons 2001 ASHA 1092-4388(2001/056); Frontiers Aging Neurosci 2022 PMC9557069), none of which contradict it; they reinforce that performance declines monotonically with compression rate for all age groups, with older adults declining more steeply. Minor nuance: the claim's "in all subjects" is a slight paraphrase of the source's "in all cases" (a condition-level statement), but the substantive meaning is identical and not an overreach. Source quality (primary peer-reviewed audiology journal) matches the modest, well-replicated claim strength. Caveat for the broader research question: the abstract does not report peripheral hearing thresholds, so audibility is not explicitly controlled in this paraphrase, though that does not undermine the specific claim made.
result.counterSource: No credible contradicting source found; corroborating sources include Gordon-Salant & Fitzgibbons (2001, JSLHR) and Frontiers Aging Neurosci 2022 (PMC9557069), both confirming monotonic decline with compression rate.

result.evidence: VERIFIED against the primary source (Goupell et al., 2022, Front. Aging Neurosci. 14:887581; PMC9557069). The supporting quote is genuine: the exact sentence "the deficits in speech recognition of older listeners for time-compressed speech may be primarily affected by age-related declines in central auditory processing rather than solely related to peripheral age-related hearing loss" appears verbatim in the paper's Summary section (the word "primarily" is the authors', not the claimant's). The study's design directly tests the claim's mechanism: it compared 46 normal-hearing (NH) listeners and 58 cochlear-implant (CI) users across young/middle/older age bands, explicitly because "the CI bypasses many of the outer, middle, and inner ear sources of age-related hearing loss." Results: the age-related decline in time-compressed-speech recognition was present in CI users (40% & 60% compression poorer for middle-aged and older CI groups vs younger CI), and there was NO significant listener-group × age-group interaction (all p>0.05), i.e., the age effect was similar in CI and NH groups — directly supporting the claim's "same age-related decline as normal-hearing listeners." A measure of cognitive processing speed did NOT predict the time-compression effect, further pointing to central AUDITORY (not generic cognitive) aging. The claim's paraphrase ("same decline") matches the paper's "age-related differences ... may be similar in both CI and NH listener groups." Broader literature (e.g., PMC6364265, PMC4627162) corroborates that older adults struggle with time-compressed speech even at normal thresholds, finding no contradiction. CAVEATS (do not rise to refutation): (a) the ABSTRACT hedges to "at least partially responsible ... rather than solely peripheral," softer than the Summary's "primarily"; the claim adopts the stronger Summary wording, which is faithful but represents the paper's least-conservative framing. (b) Authors note limitations: they did not directly measure basic auditory temporal processing and cannot fully exclude that central changes arose from pre-implant peripheral hearing loss. Source is peer-reviewed, NIH-funded, current (2022), PubMed/PMC-indexed — quality matches claim strength.
result.counterSource: No credible contradicting source found. Minor internal tension: the paper's abstract uses "at least partially" central rather than the claim's "primarily" (which is sourced from the paper's Summary section); authors acknowledge they did not directly measure temporal processing and cannot rule out pre-implant peripheral loss as a cause of central changes.

result.evidence: Primary source (PMC6238820, "Ongoing maturation in the time-compressed speech test") fully verifies every specific figure: children 8-10 yrs (mean 9±0.83, n=30), young adults 16-24 yrs (mean 21.03±2.14, n=30); 60% compression ratio; young adults significantly outperformed children on monosyllables AND disyllables in BOTH ears, all p<0.001; disyllables right ear — children mean 39.67 (SD 2.50) vs adults mean 46.70 (SD 2.02). All participants were screened for normal peripheral hearing (controls audibility) and had no central auditory processing/cognitive deficits, so this is a clean normative comparison. The interpretation ("still maturing during childhood/adolescence, higher in young adults") matches the study's own title and stated aim ("verify the neuromaturational influence in the ability of auditory closure"). Web search found NO contradicting evidence; independent sources (PubMed 893199 articulation; PMC3209666 infancy) corroborate that time-compressed speech comprehension continues maturing from childhood into young adulthood. Minor non-fatal caveats: small n (30/group), Brazilian-Portuguese sample, authors flag uncontrolled schooling/socioeconomic variables and limited generalizability — but these qualify, not refute, the appropriately-scoped claim.

VERIFIED against primary source (PMC6238820 = Lima et al., Clinics Sao Paulo, 2018, peer-reviewed, indexed in PMC). Every quantitative figure in the claim matches verbatim: children 8-10 yrs (mean 9 +/- 0.83, n=30), young adults 16-24 yrs (mean 21.03 +/- 2.14, n=30), single compression rate of 60%, monosyllabic/disyllabic word lists. Young adults significantly outperformed children on BOTH monosyllables and disyllables, p<0.001 for both ears. Disyllables right ear: children 39.67 (SD 2.50) vs adults 46.70 (SD 2.02) — exact match. Importantly, peripheral hearing was CONTROLLED: "The 60 subjects had normal peripheral hearing... without neurological, psychiatric, cognitive or central auditory processing deficits," which strengthens the central/maturational (not audibility) interpretation. The study's own conclusion supports the claim: "demonstrated the influence and impact of age (maturational factor) on TCS test performance." Source quality matches claim strength. Caveats (not refutations): small n=30/group, isolated word lists rather than connected narration, and only two cross-sectional age bands so the exact maturation age is not pinpointed — but the directional claim that time-compressed-speech ability is still maturing in childhood/adolescence and is higher in young adults is well-supported. No credible contradicting evidence found; consistent with broader developmental auditory-processing literature.

Claim is accurately supported by the primary source (PMC6238820, a peer-reviewed developmental study of children 8-10y vs young adults 16-24y, all with normal peripheral hearing screened). Verified verbatim from the article: for time-compressed speech testing, age explained 64.4% of variance (right ear) and 57.7% (left ear) for DISYLLABLES, versus 23.6% (right ear) and 21.4% (left ear) for MONOSYLLABLES — matching the claim's figures exactly. The article states age "was a strong predictor of TCST performance in the disyllable modality, and a moderate correlation was observed in the monosyllable modality" (Pearson r: disyllables 0.80/0.76; monosyllables 0.49/0.54, all p<0.001), supporting the strong/moderate characterization. The interpretive sentence is also supported: authors attribute the larger disyllable age effect to maturation of lexical-semantic and higher-level cognitive (top-down) processes, consistent with the claim that the maturational age effect is larger for stimuli requiring more top-down inference. Source is a primary peer-reviewed study, matching the claim strength; peripheral hearing was controlled. Caveat (not refuting): this is a MATURATIONAL/developmental finding (childhood-to-young-adulthood), not an aging-decline finding — the claim correctly frames it as 'maturational,' so no misattribution. No cherry-picking or misread detected.

VERIFIED against primary source PMC6238820 (Time-compressed speech test study, Clinics São Paulo, 2018, peer-reviewed). All four variance figures match exactly: disyllables 64.4% (right ear) / 57.7% (left ear); monosyllables 23.6% (right ear) / 21.4% (left ear). Correlation coefficients confirm strength characterization: disyllables r=0.80/0.76 (strong), monosyllables r=0.49/0.54 (moderate). The interpretive claim ("maturational age effect is larger for stimuli requiring more top-down cognitive inference") is the authors' OWN explanation, not an overreach: they write performance on disyllables reflects "neuromaturation of top-down factors (i.e., cognition, lexical and semantic processes and other executive functions)" and "when a large proportion of an acoustic signal is missing, the test becomes cognitive, as the individual must infer the complete message." Population: 30 children (8-10 yr) vs 30 young adults (16-24 yr), normal hearing — so this is correctly a DEVELOPMENTAL/maturational finding, not aging decline, and the claim does not misrepresent it as such. Caveats (not refuting): small n=60, single-language (Brazilian Portuguese), normal-hearing only so peripheral hearing not used as a covariate; the "more cognitively demanding" framing is a mild simplification of the authors' "more acoustic cues / extrinsic redundancy aiding top-down processing" mechanism, but the directional conclusion is faithful.

Verified against the primary source (Rabelo, Rocha-Muniz & Schochat 2018, "Ongoing maturation in the time-compressed speech test," Clinics (Sao Paulo) 73:e407, PMC6238820). All quantitative figures are verbatim accurate: the paper states age explained "64.4% of the variation in right ear performance and 57.7% of the variation in left ear performance" for disyllables, and "23.6% for the right ear and 21.4% for the left ear" for monosyllables. The strength characterization is exact: "Age was a strong predictor of TCST performance in the disyllable modality, and a moderate correlation was observed in the monosyllable modality." Pearson r values in Table 2 corroborate (disyllables RE r=0.80/LE r=0.76; monosyllables RE r=0.49/LE r=0.54, all p<0.001). The claim's mechanistic framing ("more top-down cognitive inference") is fairly attributed to the paper's own discussion, which explains disyllables provide higher acoustic cues/extrinsic redundancies enabling young adults' mature higher-level cognitive skills to "interpret and close incoming sensory information." Crucially, the claim correctly labels this a "maturational" age effect — and the study IS a developmental maturation design (30 children aged 8-10 vs 30 young adults aged 16-24, all normal-hearing, so audibility is controlled at 60% compression). No contradicting source found. CAVEATS (not refuting, but limiting generalization): (a) this is a small (n=60), single-language (Brazilian Portuguese), single-compression-ratio (60%) study; the precise variance percentages are sample-specific and should not be treated as universal constants; (b) the "age" here is the upward childhood-to-adulthood developmental curve, NOT aging decline — it speaks only to angle (1) of the research question, and the regression mixes two discrete groups, inflating apparent variance-explained relative to a continuous within-age-band analysis. The claim does not overstate this, as it explicitly uses "maturational."

result.summary: Across the lifespan, comprehension of accelerated/time-compressed speech follows a maturational rise then an aging decline, governed primarily by central auditory and cognitive factors rather than peripheral hearing. (1) Developmental curve: the brain machinery for moderate compression is canonical and adult-like at birth (newborns process 60%-compressed speech like normal-rate speech), the ability is already substantially functional by age 8, but it keeps maturing into young adulthood — young adults (16-24) significantly outperform children (8-10) on a 60%-compression test, with age explaining ~58-64% of variance for cognitively demanding disyllables versus ~21-24% for monosyllables. (2) Aging decline: deficits emerge before 65 — measurable as early as the 50s and even in mid-adulthood (15-50 normal-hearing sample) — concentrated at the hardest compression rates (50-60%, ~10 syllables/second); older adults (56-82) scored ~0.54 vs ~0.74 for younger at 60% compression, with middle-aged (50s-60s) patterning with older rather than younger listeners, and no further decline detected within the older band (62-88). (3) Mechanisms: the age penalty persists after controlling for audibility (normal thresholds ≤25 dB HL; adding thresholds did not improve models; cochlear-implant users who bypass cochlear pathology show the same age decline), and is statistically mediated by perceptuomotor/central processing speed, with auditory neural processing (P1 latency), processing speed, and executive function each contributing independently — so central/cognitive factors dominate over presbycusis. (4) Peak: maximum performance occurs in young adulthood (roughly late teens to ~30), forming a broad plateau before mid-life decline begins. (5) Trainability/plasticity: the rapid-speech difficulty is NOT inherent to aging — experienced older blind listeners comprehend rapid speech as well as young sighted adults and far better than older sighted adults, with performance correlating strongly with cumulative speed-listening exposure (r=.82 quiet, .88 noise); both early- and late-blind individuals can acquire ultra-fast (~20 syl/s) comprehension. For your healthy adult reading audiobooks at ~13 syllables/second: this already exceeds the ~10 syl/s (60% compression) that separates young from old listeners in lab studies and is firmly above the maturation/aging-sensitive range — consistent with a trained skill in the upper register, well below the ~20 syl/s ceiling that experienced blind listeners reach.

findings.claim: The age penalty for comprehending time-compressed/fast speech is independent of, and persists after controlling for, peripheral hearing loss (presbycusis); age and hearing loss operate largely separately and additively.
findings.evidence: Gordon-Salant et al. (PMC4627162): older listeners struggle with faster-than-normal rates 'independent of the effects of hearing loss'; effects 'operate independently, and therefore may become additive.' Dias et al. (PMC6364265): all participants normal-hearing (<=25 dB HL, 250-3000 Hz); adding audiometric thresholds did not improve the model (chi2(1)=0.130, p=0.718; threshold t(48)=0.350, p=0.728); 'audiometric thresholds do not mediate the relationship between age and time-compressed speech identification.' PMC6377055: older (66-76) vs younger (19-22) both normal-hearing, age penalty in noise on time-compressed (p=0.012) and natural-fast (p=0.005) speech. Kannada study (15-50, all normal-hearing): central, not peripheral. NOTE: the strict 'additive' framing is debated — some 2022 reviews favor an interaction where hearing loss accelerates the decline — but the load-bearing independence/persistence finding is robust.

findings.claim: The age effect is driven primarily by CENTRAL auditory processing and cognitive/perceptual processing speed, not peripheral audibility; it is statistically mediated by perceptuomotor processing speed, with auditory neural processing (P1 latency), processing speed, and executive function each contributing independently.
findings.evidence: Dias et al. (PMC6364265, JARO 2018): adding perceptuomotor processing speed rendered the age-group effect nonsignificant ('declines in perceptuomotor processing speed mediated age-group differences'); three factors remained simultaneously significant — P1 peak latency (beta=-0.181, p=0.004), perceptuomotor speed (beta=0.242, p=0.004), executive functioning (beta=0.132, p=0.031). Tinnemore/Goupell 2022 (PMC9557069): cochlear-implant users who bypass cochlear pathology showed the SAME age decline as normal-hearing listeners (no listener-group x age interaction), so deficits 'may be primarily affected by age-related declines in central auditory processing rather than solely related to peripheral age-related hearing loss'; notably a generic cognitive processing-speed measure did NOT predict the time-compression effect, pointing to central AUDITORY aging specifically. Kannada study: 'ageing has a deleterious effect on the performance of the central auditory processing system.' NUANCE: working-memory/phonological-loop capacity was a candidate but not isolated as the dominant mediator in these studies; processing speed and central auditory timing dominate.

findings.claim: Age-related decline in time-compressed speech comprehension begins before 65 — measurable in mid-adulthood and the 50s — is concentrated at high compression (40-60%, up to ~10 syllables/second), and middle-aged listeners pattern with older rather than younger adults.
findings.evidence: Tinnemore/Goupell 2022 (PMC9557069): middle-aged (52-64, M=59.75) and older (65+) normal-hearing groups showed similar deficits vs younger, 'no significant differences between MNH and ONH groups for 40 and 60% time-compressed speech'; age effects 'may affect performance by adults prior to 65 years of age... as young as 50 years old (or younger).' Compression-to-rate mapping: 0/20/40/60% ~= 3.7/4.6/6.4/10 syllables/second; performance at ceiling for 0-20%, declining from 40%. Dias et al. (PMC6364265): older (56-82) vs younger (19-30) — 60% compression 0.537 vs 0.740 (t(49)=4.859, p<0.001), 50% 0.795 vs 0.868 (t(49)=2.725, p=0.009), NO age difference at 40%. Kannada (15-50): young adults beat middle-aged on time-compressed speech, decline within mid-adulthood even without hearing loss.

findings.claim: Older adults are progressively more impaired by increasing compression rate, demonstrated across 30-60% compression; the deficit differentiates middle-aged from older adults, but does NOT clearly worsen further within the older band (roughly 62-88).
findings.evidence: Gordon-Salant et al. (PMC4627162): significant main effects of age, hearing loss, and time-compression ratio across 30/40/50/60%, with no interactions (consistent age deficit at every ratio). Letowski & Poch 1996 (PMID 8972446): older (60-69) affected more by compression rate and discard interval than middle-aged (42-54); 'time-compressed speech differentiates between speech comprehension by middle-aged and older adults'; performance deteriorated with increasing compression in all subjects. Jenstad & Souza 2007 (ASHA, mild-moderate hearing loss): NO difference between young-old (62-74, n=11) and old-old (75-88, n=14) on 50%-compressed low-predictability sentences, no age interactions — suggesting the steepest behavioral transition is younger-vs-older rather than continuous decline deep into old age. CAVEAT: the null within-older-band result is from a small, possibly underpowered sample.

findings.claim: Comprehension of moderately time-compressed speech is supported by canonical, adult-like brain responses at birth and is already substantially functional by age 8, but continues to mature into young adulthood — especially for cognitively demanding stimuli requiring top-down inference.
findings.evidence: Gervain et al. (PMC6987815): newborns (mean 2.34 days) process 60%-compressed speech with the same canonical hemodynamic response as normal-rate speech (no significant difference), while 30% compression inverts — 'adult-like processing... by newborns,' adaptation 'requires little or no experience.' Dispaldro et al. (PMC5786310): in typically-developing children 8-13, age did not significantly predict fast/time-compressed speech performance (already functional, though no fine trajectory stratified; small n=16). Lemos et al. (PMC6238820): young adults (16-24, M=21) significantly outperform children (8-10, M=9) at 60% compression on monosyllables AND disyllables (all p<0.001; disyllables right ear ~46.70 vs ~39.67); age explained 64.4%/57.7% of disyllable variance vs only 23.6%/21.4% for monosyllables — maturation is larger for top-down/lexical-semantic demands, indicating continued maturation through childhood/adolescence into young adulthood.

findings.claim: Peak accelerated-speech comprehension is reached in young adulthood (roughly late teens through ~30), forming a broad plateau before mid-life decline; this is the maximum of the lifespan curve.
findings.evidence: Convergent inference from the developmental and aging literatures: young adults (16-24, Lemos PMC6238820; 19-30, Dias PMC6364265; 19-23, Tinnemore/Goupell PMC9557069) form the high-performing reference group, outperforming both children and middle-aged/older adults, while no decline is yet evident at 40% compression for the under-30s. The plateau runs from the end of maturation (late teens / early 20s) to the start of mid-life decline detectable in the 50s (and middle-aged 52-64 already patterning with older), implying a roughly two-to-three-decade plateau peaking in the 20s. NOTE: no single study pinpoints an exact peak age; this is an integration of maturation-completion and decline-onset boundaries rather than a directly measured peak.
findings.vote: derived synthesis (boundaries from claims 14,15,19; no contradicting evidence)

findings.claim: The rapid-speech difficulty of older adults is TRAINABLE, not an inherent consequence of aging: experienced older blind listeners match young sighted adults and far exceed older sighted adults, with a strong dose-response to cumulative speed-listening exposure; both early- and late-blind individuals can acquire ultra-fast (~20 syl/s) comprehension.
findings.evidence: Gordon-Salant & Friedman (via PMC4627162): older blind participants performed significantly better than older sighted in all time-compression conditions in quiet, with NO difference between older blind and younger sighted in any condition; difficulty with rapid speech 'is not inherent to the aging process, but rather, can be ameliorated through training and experience.' Strong dose-response: performance correlated r=.82 (quiet) and r=.88 (noise, p<.01) with hours/week spent listening to faster-than-normal-rate materials (books on tape). The review notes both early- and late-blind acquire ~20 syl/s comprehension, indicating experience-driven (not congenital-only) acquisition and visual-cortex plasticity recruited for fast-speech in blindness. CAVEATS: the blind comparison is cross-sectional/correlational (small n=10), not a randomized training trial, so blindness-related cortical plasticity is not fully separable from training; older-blind advantage is largest in quiet and attenuated in noise; older-adult training gains show limited generalization to untrained stimuli.

result.caveats: SCOPE/MEASUREMENT: Most studies measure WORD/SENTENCE RECOGNITION ACCURACY of time-compressed speech, not comprehension of connected audiobook narration; the ~13 syl/s the user achieves is a sustained habituated rate, whereas lab tasks (peaking ~10 syl/s at 60% compression) measure first-exposure recognition without practice — so lab thresholds understate what a trained listener can comprehend. AUDIBILITY CONTROL VARIES: the strongest mechanistic claims (Dias PMC6364265, Tinnemore/Goupell PMC9557069, PMC6377055, Kannada study) screened to normal hearing (≤25 dB HL); the older Letowski & Poch 1996 study did NOT fully control presbycusis, and 'normal-hearing' screening (≤25 dB HL, ≤4 kHz) does not exclude extended-high-frequency loss or cochlear synaptopathy, so 'central not peripheral' is a logically imperfect inference even where stated. ADDITIVE-vs-INTERACTIVE: whether hearing loss simply adds to or actively accelerates the age penalty is unresolved (2022 reviews lean toward interaction). WORKING MEMORY: explicitly named in the question but NOT isolated as a dominant mediator in the verified primary studies — processing speed and central auditory timing dominate the evidence; working-memory's specific contribution is comparatively under-evidenced here. WITHIN-OLD-BAND NULL: the 'no further decline 62-88' finding rests on one small (n=11/14), possibly underpowered 2007 study. BLIND/PLASTICITY: the ultra-fast-speech and early-vs-late-blind neural (visual-cortex) plasticity details rest substantially on a single review's summary and correlational data (n=10), not randomized trials; the precise quantitative difference between early-blind and late-blind ultra-fast ceilings was not cleanly resolved in the surviving claims. SAMPLE GAPS: the Kannada study covers only 15-50 (no 60s-80s); several studies cap compression at 60% (~10 syl/s), so behavior above ~10-13 syl/s in aging is extrapolated, not directly measured. REFUTED: claims that newborn adaptation proves the machinery is fully 'innate/abstract-linguistic,' and that central temporal processing is non-adult-like until ~13, were rejected in verification and are NOT relied upon.
openQuestions: What is the exact quantitative difference in the ultra-fast-speech ceiling and visual-cortex (occipital) plasticity between EARLY-blind and LATE-blind listeners — does age of blindness onset set a hard limit, or only modulate the rate/degree of acquisition?

By how much (effect size, syllables/second gained) can a healthy older adult improve speed-listening through structured training, and how durable and generalizable are those gains beyond trained materials?

Does the specific contribution of working-memory / phonological-loop capacity (as distinct from general processing speed and central auditory timing) become the dominant limiter at very high rates (>13-20 syl/s), where the user is operating?

Is the within-older-band stability (no decline 62-88) real, or an artifact of small samples and floor effects — does comprehension continue to decline through the 80s and 90s when adequately powered and when peripheral hearing is rigorously matched?
